title: Interplay of Nutrients, Temperature, and Competition of Native and Alien Cyanobacteria Species Growth and Cyanotoxin Production in Temperate Lakes
what: A laboratory experimental study (Toxins, 2021) using eight cyanobacterial strains isolated from four Lithuanian lakes that ran two controlled experiments — a nutrient (phosphorus concentration × N:P ratio) manipulation measuring growth rate and cyanotoxin (microcystin, saxitoxin, non-ribosomal peptide) production, and

claims.c: Under nutrient manipulation, native Aphanizomenon gracile and alien Sphaerospermopsis aphanizomenoides grew fastest of the four tested strains, while native Planktothrix agardhii grew slowest (and most variably).
P. agardhii's own maximum growth rate required both the highest phosphorus level tested and the highest N:P ratio tested.
Phosphorus (IP) and nitrogen (IN) concentration were significant GLM factors for growth rate of P. agardhii, A. gracile, and S. aphanizomenoides, but not for C. bergii.
No statistically significant relationship was found between A. gracile's growth rate, phosphorus concentration, or N:P ratio and its saxitoxin (STX) concentration.

In the toxic P. agardhii strain, phosphorus concentration was strongly negatively correlated with several microcystin/non-ribosomal-peptide variants (dmMC-RR, APs, CPs) — i.e., lower phosphorus was associated with higher toxin content per unit biomass.
A nontoxic (non-microcystin-producing) P. agardhii strain contained markedly higher concentrations of other bioactive oligopeptides than the microcystin-producing (toxic) strain.
In the interspecies competition experiment, native A. gracile achieved substantially higher biomass than native P. agardhii when co-cultured under high-nutrient, warmed (24°C) conditions.
Alien S. aphanizomenoides suppressed native P. agardhii's biomass more strongly than the reverse, especially under warmed, nutrient-enriched conditions.
Native A. gracile suppressed the biomass of both alien species (S. aphanizomenoides and C. bergii) in co-culture relative to their monoculture controls.
The authors characterize S. aphanizomenoides as a stronger invader of the two alien species tested, attributing this to broader nutrient/temperature tolerance (ecological plasticity).
Overall, native A. gracile had a suppressive (negative) effect on the biomass of both alien species and of native P. agardhii across the competition experiment.
Nutrient (IP/IN) concentration had the strongest effect on competition outcomes, followed by temperature and then species origin/identity of the competitor.

The authors state that insufficient biomass produced by P. agardhii strains at lower phosphorus concentrations limited their ability to draw firm conclusions about how nutrients affect microcystin and non-ribosomal-peptide production.

The authors state that scientific understanding of what regulates saxitoxin production in cyanobacteria, and of the mechanisms underlying interspecies competition among bloom-forming cyanobacteria, remains limited, and call for more complex, multi-stressor/multi-species experiments.

title: Non-Nitrogen-Fixers or Nitrogen-Fixers? Factors Distinguishing the Dominance of Chroococcal and Diazotrophic Cyanobacterial Species
what: A 2022 field study of four small, shallow (max depth 4 m), eutrophic water bodies near Kraków, Poland, in which paired upper- and bottom-layer water chemistry (temperature, oxygen saturation, PO4^3-, NO3-, NH4+, SO4^2-, total dissolved iron) from 62 sampling events (May-October) was analyzed with decision-tree classifi

claims.c: Sulfate (SO4^2-) concentration was the single most important factor distinguishing whether non-nitrogen-fixing or nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria dominated, ranking above water temperature, phosphate, and oxygen saturation in both the upper- and bottom-layer decision-tree models.

In the upper water layer, sulfate above 69 mg/L was associated with dominance by non-nitrogen-fixing (chroococcal) taxa, while sulfate below that level was associated with nitrogen-fixing (diazotrophic) dominance.

In the bottom layer, Microcystis spp. (non-nitrogen-fixing) dominance occurred under a combination of sulfate >73 mg/L, a PO4:NH4 ratio >0.051, and oxygen saturation >7.5%, a combination present in 12% of samples.

Across the 62 sampling events, nitrogen-fixing taxa were the dominant cyanobacterium far more often than non-nitrogen-fixing taxa (57% vs. 17% of samples), with no clearly dominant species in the remaining samples (26%).

The authors' proposed mechanism is that sulfate inhibits molybdate uptake, and because molybdenum is a required cofactor for nitrogenase (N2-fixation) enzymes, elevated sulfate suppresses nitrogen fixation and thereby disadvantages diazotrophic cyanobacteria relative to non-fixers.
Total dissolved iron (TDFe), despite being one of the five candidate environmental drivers measured, was found not to meaningfully distinguish which cyanobacteria group dominated.

The authors' own sulfate-molybdenum limitation mechanism is not universally supported in the literature: they cite a contradicting study reporting no molybdenum limitation of nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria in a different set of eutrophic saline lakes, and flag their own explanation as potentially questionable as a result.
The authors explicitly characterize their causal explanation as partly speculative because they did not directly measure nitrogen-fixation rates or nutrient uptake in this study.

The statistical approach used classification (decision) trees (R package 'rpart'), fit separately on upper-layer and bottom-layer predictor sets, to classify which cyanobacterium taxon dominated at each of the 62 sampling events; tree depth was manually restricted to reduce overfitting risk.

The upper-layer decision-tree model correctly explained the dominant taxon in 74% of samples, and the bottom-layer model in 76% of samples — i.e., roughly a quarter of samples were not correctly classified by either layer's model.

The dataset comprised 62 total paired (upper 1 m + near-bottom ~10 cm above sediment) water samples collected May-October from four shallow, eutrophic water bodies near Kraków, Poland, sampled monthly outside bloom periods and weekly during blooms.

Prior laboratory culture experiments (cited from earlier literature, not this study's own field data) have shown that Aphanizomenon flos-aquae growth does not occur at or below 11°C, with a preference for higher temperatures (~20-25°C).

Within each layer's decision tree, A. flos-aquae dominance was recovered under two different combinations of environmental variables rather than one, which the authors interpret (speculatively) as possible evidence of different physiological strains of the species.

title: On the Chemistry, Toxicology and Genetics of the Cyanobacterial Toxins, Microcystin, Nodularin, Saxitoxin and Cylindrospermopsin
what: A 2010 open-access, peer-reviewed review article in Marine Drugs (Pearson, Mihali, Moffitt, Kellmann & Neilan) that synthesizes roughly a decade of genetic and biochemical research to summarize the chemistry (structural variants), toxicology (mechanism of action, potency, and health effects), and genetics/biosynthesis

claims.c: Cyanobacterial toxins fall into four toxicological classes—neurotoxins, hepatotoxins, cytotoxins, and dermatoxins (irritant toxins)—despite being structurally very diverse.
Microcystin is the largest and most chemically diverse cyanotoxin class: a monocyclic heptapeptide with roughly 90 known structural isoforms, the most common being microcystin-LR (MW 995.17).

Microcystin-LR is markedly more acutely lethal to mice than the rarer variant microcystin-RR (intraperitoneal LD50 50 µg/kg vs. 600 µg/kg), and both act by inhibiting serine/threonine protein phosphatases 1 and 2A.

The review raises a hypothesis-level (not proven-in-humans) concern that chronic, sub-lethal microcystin exposure could contribute to cancer risk, based on laboratory rodent studies showing tumor promotion.

The microcystin biosynthesis gene cluster (mcyS: 55 kb, 10 genes in two divergent operons mcyA–C and mcyD–J, sequenced from Microcystis aeruginosa PCC7806) was the first complete secondary-metabolite gene cluster ever fully sequenced from a cyanobacterium.

Microcystin gene transcription responds to light in a threshold-like way (distinct dark/low-light vs. medium/high-light regimes), and high light also shifts the toxin-variant mixture toward the more toxic DeLR isoform.

Nodularin (cyclic pentapeptide, MW 619, 7 known natural isoforms) inhibits PP1/PP2A at a similar potency to microcystin (IC50 ≈ 0.1 nM) but binds PP2A non-covalently rather than covalently, which the authors hypothesize may explain its additional carcinogenic potential relative to microcystin.

The nodularin biosynthesis gene cluster (ndaS: 48 kb, 9 ORFs, ndaA–I) was sequenced from Nodularia spumigena NSOR10 in 2004; its tailoring enzyme NdaH shares 71% sequence identity with the microcystin enzyme McyI.

Saxitoxin and its more than 30 natural derivatives are potent voltage-gated sodium-channel blockers; in mice the intraperitoneal LD50 is 10 µg/kg, the estimated human lethal dose is as little as 1 mg, and the toxin causes an estimated 2,000 paralytic shellfish poisoning (PSP) cases worldwide per year with a 15% mortality rate.

The saxitoxin (sxt) biosynthesis gene cluster spans more than 35 kb (26 proteins/30 catalytic functions) in Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii T3 and a somewhat smaller ~28 kb in Anabaena circinalis and Aphanizomenon sp.; phylogenetic analysis of the O-carbamoyltransferase gene suggests the cluster's most likely evolutionary origin is an ancestral alpha-proteobacterium, subsequently spread by horizontal gene transfer.

Whether saxitoxin-producing eukaryotes (e.g., puffer fish, the Panamanian golden frog) actually biosynthesize the toxin themselves or merely bioaccumulate it from other organisms is explicitly unresolved in the literature the review surveys.

Some published claims of bacterial (non-dinoflagellate) saxitoxin biosynthesis are considered controversial because the analytical methods used to support them were not definitive and could not be independently reproduced.

Cylindrospermopsin — a polyketide-derived alkaloid classed as hepatotoxic, cytotoxic and neurotoxic, and considered a potential carcinogen — caused a well-documented 1979 human poisoning outbreak on Palm Island, Queensland, Australia, hospitalizing 148 people with hepatoenteritis after a bloom of Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii contaminated a drinking-water reservoir.

The cylindrospermopsin biosynthesis gene cluster (cyr: 43 kb, 15 ORFs, sequenced from C. raciborskii AWT205) includes a proposed—but not fully confirmed—novel pyrimidine-biosynthesis mechanism for forming the toxin's uracil ring, a step the authors state had previously been unexplained.
The review states that research on microcystin (hepatotoxin) regulation lacks standardized experimental growth controls across studies, which it flags as a methodological weakness of that sub-field.

title: Perceived global increase in algal blooms is attributable to intensified monitoring and emerging bloom impacts
what: A global statistical meta-analysis (Hallegraeff et al. 2021, Communications Earth & Environment) that tests whether reported harmful algal bloom (HAB) events actually increased worldwide from 1985–2018, by combining the Harmful Algae Event Database (HAEDAT) with Ocean Biodiversity Information System (OBIS) occurrence r

claims.c: There is no statistically significant uniform global increasing trend in HAB events over 1985–2018 once data are adjusted for regional differences in monitoring/sampling effort.
The raw (unadjusted) HAEDAT event count does show a statistically significant increase globally over the study period.
After adjusting for OBIS-derived sampling effort, only a subset of regions show real increases or decreases; the pattern is not globally uniform.

Adjusting for sampling effort actually flips the direction (sign) of the trend in several regions compared to the raw/unadjusted data, demonstrating that monitoring effort is a real, non-trivial confounder rather than a minor correction.
Global aquaculture production grew roughly 16-fold between 1985 and 2018, and this growth correlates strongly and significantly with the number of reported HAB events.
The authors conclude there is no empirical support for broad, undifferentiated statements that HABs are increasing globally; any trend claims must be qualified by region and species.
The perceived global increase in HAB events is attributed largely to intensified observational/monitoring effort rather than a demonstrated uniform ecological increase.
Intensified monitoring tied to aquaculture growth, not necessarily climate change or nutrient pollution, is presented as the best-supported explanation for the perceived increase.

Data quality and monitoring coverage were themselves highly uneven over the study window: consistent phytoplankton/seafood-toxin monitoring only began in most regions in 1985–1990s, and in North Asia only from 2000, meaning the analyzed period partly captures monitoring-system build-out rather than a stable observation baseline.
Some regional datasets were too sparse to support trend analysis at all and were excluded from the formal statistics.

The dataset comprises 9,503 total HAEDAT events (as of the data cutoff), and OBIS sampling-effort proxy data comprise 5,944,392 microalgal records including 289,668 harmful-species distribution records.

Data prior to 1985 are too sparse and unreliable to determine whether a real HAB increase might already have been underway before the analyzed period, so the paper's 'no global trend' finding is explicitly bounded to 1985–2018 and cannot be extended backward.

It is an important methodological caution

title: Predicting Cyanobacteria dominance in lakes
what: A short (~4-page) peer-reviewed paper in the Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences (2001) that compiles data from 99 well-studied temperate-zone lakes to test whether the nitrogen-to-phosphorus (N:P) ratio, versus absolute total phosphorus, total nitrogen, or standing algal biomass, better predicts cyanoba

claims.c: The paper explicitly frames itself as challenging an established ecological precept: that low N:P ratios cause noxious/toxic cyanobacteria blooms, a precept it says rests on prior observational and lab/field-experimental evidence.
Using a compiled dataset of 99 well-studied temperate-zone lakes, the authors conclude the N:P-ratio model of cyanobacteria dominance is flawed.

Water-quality degradation risk from cyanobacteria blooms correlates more strongly with variation in total phosphorus (TP), total nitrogen (TN), or standing algal biomass than with the N:P ratio itself.
The authors conclude cyanobacteria-associated risk is better explained by simple increases in nutrient concentration and algal biomass than by the N:P ratio.

The paper motivates its topic by framing cyanobacteria dominance as a major human and ecosystem health risk, which is why stoichiometric (N:P) control had become central to freshwater resource management prior to this study.

title: Rapid flotation of Microcystis wesenbergii mediated by high light exposure: implications for surface scum formation and cyanobacterial species succession
what: A laboratory experimental study comparing two single-cell Microcystis strains (M. wesenbergii FACHB-908 and M. aeruginosa FACHB-905) under high light to identify a non-buoyancy-driven, hours-scale flotation mechanism (photosynthetic micro-bubble trapping plus EPS-mediated cell aggregation) that produces rapid surface s

claims.c: Under high light (150 μmol photons s⁻¹ m⁻²), M. wesenbergii migrated upward rapidly and formed visible surface scum within 4 hours, whereas M. aeruginosa did not form visible scum for the duration of the experiment.

The rapid flotation is associated with photosynthesis-driven dissolved-oxygen (DO) supersaturation and formation of micro-bubbles (50–200 µm diameter) that become trapped in/on cell aggregates, providing lift to the surface.
DO rose far faster under light in M. wesenbergii (5 mg L⁻¹ h⁻¹) than in M. aeruginosa (2 mg L⁻¹ h⁻¹), with M. wesenbergii reaching the DO probe's detection limit (42 mg/L) within about 300 minutes.

M. wesenbergii secreted significantly more extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) - both dissolved and bound polysaccharides and proteins - than M. aeruginosa, and this EPS is proposed to mediate cell-cell adhesion via a 'bridging' network of functional groups.

Zeta potential of M. wesenbergii cell suspensions became markedly less negative over the light exposure (from below −30 mV toward about −5 mV), consistent with reduced electrostatic repulsion enabling aggregation, while M. aeruginosa showed no significant zeta-potential change.

M. wesenbergii's surface-layer samples had much higher polysaccharide concentrations (dissolved 41.8 mg/L, bound 72.2 mg/L) than its mixed/water-column samples (dissolved 10.7 mg/L, bound 6.6 mg/L), whereas M. aeruginosa showed no significant difference in polysaccharide content between surface and water-column samples.

The observed hours-scale scum formation is faster than the days-to-weeks timescale typically described for conventional buoyancy (gas-vesicle)-driven Microcystis bloom/scum formation, implying an additional mechanism operates under strong light.

The authors conclude the mechanism is species-specific and propose it has implications for surface scum formation and succession among cyanobacterial species (i.e., which Microcystis species/morphotypes come to dominate a bloom's surface layer).

The authors' three-part conclusion is that (a) EPS from M. wesenbergii contributes to large aggregate formation, (b) aggregate plus micro-bubble formation together drive surface scum formation, and (c) light and EPS jointly contribute to formation of the large algal aggregate.

The authors explicitly caveat that their experiments used laboratory strains under simplified indoor conditions and that the mechanism still needs to be tested in natural samples/populations, which may behave differently (e.g., environmental M. wesenbergii forms large colonies with complex vertical-migration dynamics).

title: Recent Advances in the Ecology of Bloom-Forming Raphidiopsis (Cylindrospermopsis) raciborskii: Expansion in China, Intraspecific Heterogeneity and Critical Factors for Invasion
what: A 2023 open-access narrative literature review (Int. J. Environ. Res. Public Health 20(3):1984, DOI 10.3390/ijerph20031984) that synthesizes published research on the ecology and invasion of the bloom-forming, toxin-producing cyanobacterium Raphidiopsis (Cylindrospermopsis) raciborskii in China. It focuses on the speci

claims.c: R. raciborskii has expanded from tropical/subtropical China into temperate zones over recent decades and is now documented across a wide latitudinal/longitudinal range spanning eight major river basins.
The first reported R. raciborskii bloom in China occurred in 2002 in the Pearl River (Zhujiang) delta area.
In Liangzi Lake (Hubei), R. raciborskii biomass rose from a low background level first noted in 2006 to a bloom level of 52.03 mg/L during 2013–2017.
The Haihe River basin currently marks the northernmost documented limit of R. raciborskii presence in China.

R. raciborskii dominance and blooms have been observed in relatively cool water (10-15 °C), and there is marked strain-to-strain variation in cold tolerance: some/several strains sustain high growth at 15 °C while others are completely growth-inhibited at similarly low temperatures.

Competition outcomes between R. raciborskii and Microcystis aeruginosa are highly strain-dependent and vary with experimental conditions (temperature, phosphorus source, light limitation, pH/inorganic carbon), with no single consistent winner across studies.

R. raciborskii is a major producer of the hepatotoxin cylindrospermopsin (CYN); Chinese isolates show genetic variation in the CYN-synthesis genes cyrI (4 main sequence types) and cyrJ (3 main sequence types), with lower-latitude reservoirs harboring more sequence types, consistent with a southeast-China diversity/origin center.

Toxigenic strains producing paralytic shellfish poison (PSP, i.e., saxitoxin-group toxins) have also been isolated and identified from Chinese R. raciborskii populations, and these are phylogenetically close to strains from North and South America.

R. raciborskii can dominate under both phosphorus-limited and nitrogen-limited/fluctuating conditions via a physiological toolkit: strong P storage plus alkaline-phosphatase-mediated use of dissolved organic phosphorus under P limitation, and N2 fixation plus cyanophycin N storage under N depletion; the review states it can dominate at both very low and high N:P ratios, but gives no specific concentration or ratio thresholds.

Selective grazing pressure affects R. raciborskii establishment: Daphnia grazing on R. raciborskii can suppress its population, whereas copepods that preferentially graze on co-occurring non-Raphidiopsis taxa can indirectly favor R. raciborskii persistence.

The authors argue that a 'microevolution' framework (ongoing fine-grained genetic/phenotypic diversification) explains the coexistence of different R. raciborskii phenotypes/genotypes within a single environment better than the traditional 'ecotype' concept, and propose this intraspecific heterogeneity as a driving force behind the species' geographic expansion.

R. raciborskii has spread among China's disconnected water bodies via multiple physical dispersal pathways, including engineered water-diversion projects — e.g., a project linking five lakes in the Huaihe River basin with three reservoirs — plus river flow, flood-linked floodplain lakes, ship ballast water, aquatic-product transport water, and migratory birds carrying akinetes.

title: Roles of Nutrient Limitation on Western Lake Erie CyanoHAB Toxin Production
what: A 2021 peer-reviewed experimental study (Barnard et al., Toxins 13(1):47) that ran in situ nutrient-addition and 40%-dilution microcosm bioassays on natural Western Lake Erie bloom water from Maumee Bay (Microcystis-dominated) and Sandusky Bay (Planktothrix-dominated) in June and August 2019, to test whether nitrogen (

claims.c: In June, Maumee Bay bloom growth was phosphorus-limited (not nitrogen-limited) despite very high ambient nitrate, and the 40% dilution treatment made no significant difference to growth; cyanotoxins were not detected at this site/time.

In June, Sandusky Bay (Planktothrix) growth was nutrient-replete, but biomass, microcystin, and anatoxin production were jointly (co-)limited by N and P, and microcystin production shifted to nutrient-deplete once nutrients were cut via 40% dilution.
In August, the Maumee Bay Microcystis bloom produced microcystin under nutrient-replete conditions, with only a slight, induced phosphorus limitation appearing under the 40% dilution treatment.

In August, the Sandusky Bay Planktothrix bloom's anatoxin production was primarily nitrogen-limited in both the undiluted and 40%-diluted treatments, and nitrate additions raised anatoxin concentrations more than ammonium or urea additions did.
The authors conclude their results contradict the older assumption that phosphorus alone controls Western Lake Erie CyanoHABs, and argue for a paradigm shift toward also managing nitrogen inputs.
The paper's explicit watershed-management recommendation is that both nitrogen and phosphorus reduction efforts should be pursued, not phosphorus reduction alone.

Simulating a 40% nutrient-load reduction (via dilution) produced only a slight reduction in biomass and toxin (microcystin/anatoxin) production in these eutrophic bays, leading the authors to warn that the 40% target agreed between the US and Canada might not be sufficient in such nutrient-saturated bay environments.

Because the ratio of microcystin to chlorophyll a was nearly linear across both June and August, the authors infer that the dominant bloom-forming taxa themselves (Planktothrix in Sandusky Bay, Microcystis in Maumee Bay) — rather than a rare secondary strain — were the main microcystin producers; this is an inference from a correlational (biomass-toxin) pattern, not a direct causal test of which taxon produced the toxin.

Even though microcystin's synthesis pathway is nitrogen-linked, the experiments show phosphorus is independently required to reach higher microcystin concentrations, i.e., microcystin output is not governed by nitrogen availability alone.

The study reports anatoxin-a (plus dihydro- and homoanatoxin-a) production associated with the Sandusky Bay Planktothrix bloom, which the authors present as new documentation of anatoxin production in Lake Erie and use to argue this neurotoxin warrants closer monitoring.

title: Schindler's legacy: from eutrophic lakes to the phosphorus utilization strategies of cyanobacteria
what: A peer-reviewed literature review (FEMS Microbiology Reviews, Nov 2022, DOI 10.1093/femsre/fuac029) that frames itself as a ~50-year update to David Schindler's 1970s whole-lake research establishing phosphorus as a driver of cyanobacterial blooms, synthesizing physiological, molecular/genomic, and modelling literature

claims.c: The review is explicitly framed as a ~50-year update to David Schindler's 1970s whole-lake studies that established phosphorus as a trigger of cyanobacterial blooms in North American lakes, and the authors note that nutrient pollution has continued despite that early evidence.

Bioavailable phosphate can occur at extremely low, near-detection-limit concentrations in lake surface waters, yet phytoplankton uptake rates can still be high at these low concentrations, illustrating that standard chemical P measurement poorly captures physiologically relevant bioavailable P.

Cyanobacteria-dominated assemblages show measurable threshold/transition Pi concentrations governing uptake kinetics that vary by species: about 0.84–2.74 µg P/L for a Planktothrix agardhii/Raphidiopsis mediterranea-dominated population, and a shift from low- to high-affinity uptake at about 4.75 µg P/L for Raphidiopsis raciborskii from a natural lake population.

Cyanobacteria possess at least two phosphate transport systems - the constitutive Pit system (PitA/PitB) and the Pho-regulon-controlled, ATP-driven Pst system (with duplicate Pst1/Pst2 gene copies in some freshwater cyanobacterial genomes) - and the article states that PitA's affinity for phosphate is about two orders of magnitude greater than that of the Pst system.

Cyanobacteria perform 'luxury' phosphorus storage, accumulating intracellular polyphosphate (polyP) beyond immediate needs when Pi is abundant relative to N/S or after resupply following P starvation; cellular P quotas can differ roughly six-fold between P-replete and P-starved culture conditions in Raphidiopsis raciborskii.

Cyanobacteria scavenge phosphate from dissolved organic phosphorus (DOP) via phosphohydrolase enzymes (alkaline phosphatase genes phoA/phoD/phoX, acid phosphatases, 5'-nucleotidase, phosphonatases/C-P lyase), and measured phosphatase activity increases sharply and in a species-specific way under phosphate-depleted conditions: 226-fold and 225-fold in cyanobacteria-dominated field assemblages, 6.4-8.0-fold in Dolichospermum flos-aquae, and 54-fold in Nodularia spumigena.

Standard analytical methods for measuring phosphorus (colorimetric orthophosphate assays; DOP estimated by subtraction) are only proxies for the biologically available P pool and can misrepresent it, particularly at low concentrations, with subtraction-based DOP estimates especially error-prone as the difference approaches zero.

A minimum cellular phosphorus quota (about 0.3-0.45 pg P per cell) is required for akinete (dormant resting-cell) formation in Dolichospermum circinale, linking cell-level P status to a specific cyanobacterial life-cycle/survival transition.

The review concludes that despite decades of physiological measurement, understanding of what drives cyanobacterial species diversity, adaptation, and competitive dominance under low-phosphorus conditions remains limited, and calls for interdisciplinary integration of physiology, molecular biology, and modelling to improve prediction and mitigation of toxic blooms.

The review argues that P-related physiological traits are under-recognized relative to other commonly cited cyanobacterial bloom-dominance traits, noting that species differ markedly in P-scavenging/storage strategy (e.g., Microcystis upregulating P-scavenging genes such as pstSCAB/phoX under low P, versus Dolichospermum triggering nitrogen-fixation genes under high-P conditions).

title: Selection of photosynthetic traits by turbulent mixing governs formation of cyanobacterial blooms in shallow eutrophic lakes
what: A numerical modeling study (The ISME Journal, vol 18, issue 1, article wrae021, published 2 Feb 2024, DOI 10.1093/ismejo/wrae021) that couples a one-dimensional hydrodynamic model with a trait-based phytoplankton model to test whether intraspecific variation in photosynthetic capacity (Pmax) within Microcystis populati

claims.c: Turbulence acts as a directional selective driver on the Pmax composition of a Microcystis population: depending on the intensity of daily-periodic (wind-driven) turbulence, the population-averaged phenotype shifts toward either low Pmax (favoring light capture in upper layers) or high Pmax (favoring efficient light use).

Moderate turbulence produces a stable, near-total dominance of the lowest-Pmax trait group (g1) — its relative cell density exceeded 99.9% under Dz,max = 1×10⁻⁴ m² s⁻¹ — whereas stronger turbulence (Dz,max = 1×10⁻³ m² s⁻¹) preserved substantially more trait diversity (other trait groups g2–g10 collectively reaching roughly 8% or more).
Populations with wider intraspecific Pmax diversity form severe surface scum up to more than four times faster than populations with narrower Pmax diversity, all else equal.

Higher background turbidity speeds up the onset of decline in population-averaged Pmax (time-to-onset fell from 40 days at Kbg=0.6 m⁻¹, to 12 days at 1.2 m⁻¹, to 7 days at 1.8 m⁻¹) but delays the later reversal/recovery of Pmax, with high turbidity delaying the reversal point by a factor of 2.5 relative to low turbidity.

Across all 126 simulated scenarios, the time needed to reach a moderate or severe bloom threshold ranged from 26 days up to more than 180 days (i.e., some parameter combinations never crossed the threshold within the simulated period); under the most favorable condition (low turbulence, low turbidity) all six populations reached a moderate bloom within a similar, narrow 26–27 day window regardless of their Pmax diversity.

The model architecture couples (i) a one-dimensional hydrodynamic model, (ii) an ensemble-averaged transport model for trait-specific cell density/colony size/cell-tissue density, and (iii) an ecological (photosynthesis/growth) model; it was run for 6 Microcystis populations (each split into 10 Pmax trait groups, g1-g10) x 7 turbulence levels x 3 turbidity levels = 126 total 180-day simulations, implemented in MATLAB 2022b.

The authors propose that controlled, moderate artificial mixing applied during a bloom event could reduce the diversity of photosynthetic capacity within the population, lower its resilience, and thereby help mitigate future blooms — framed as a model-derived management hypothesis, not a field-tested intervention.

The model assumes Microcystis growth depends solely on irradiance, holds Pmax fixed within each trait group (no phenotypic plasticity), and neglects temperature effects, nutrient limitation, cell adhesion/colony disaggregation, and other traits (e.g., toxigenicity, variable optimal light intensity); the authors state it does not capture all processes that influence real bloom dynamics.

title: The PEG-model of seasonal succession of planktonic events in fresh waters
what: A foundational, working-group-authored limnology paper (by the Plankton Ecology Group, a working group of SIL affiliated since ~1977) that proposes a 24-statement verbal model of the idealized seasonal succession of phytoplankton and zooplankton in a "standard" temperate lake patterned on Lake Constance, then tests tha

claims.c: The paper proposes a verbal model of 24 sequential statements describing, step by step, the seasonal succession of phytoplankton and zooplankton in an idealized 'standard' lake modeled on Lake Constance.
The 24 model statements were tested ('confronted') against real seasonal-succession data from 24 different lakes, reservoirs, and ponds studied by named PEG members.
Comparing the model to empirical data revealed a major dichotomy in successional pathways between lakes with versus without elevated summer algal biomass.

This succession-pathway dichotomy, along with features such as grazing pressure, is offered as a comparison/alternative lens to the traditional classification of water bodies by trophic status (eutrophic vs. oligotrophic).
The paper frames plankton succession as fundamentally predictable and directional, but explicitly allows that irregular physical events can disturb this predictability.

A central mechanism described in the model is a mid-succession 'clear-water phase': phytoplankton biomass crashes to a low equilibrium once herbivore (zooplankton) grazing pressure exceeds algal growth, persisting until less-edible/inedible algae develop.

Later statements in the model link a sequence of nutrient limitations (phosphate, then silica, then nitrogen) to shifts in dominant phytoplankton groups, with nitrogen depletion favoring nitrogen-fixing filamentous cyanobacteria ('blue-green algae') — a mechanism directly relevant to why harmful cyanobacterial blooms tend to emerge later in the seasonal succession.

The paper's four named authors represent a much larger set of contributing PEG members (a secondary analysis of the paper's acknowledgements footnote counted roughly 25-29 additional names), reflecting that the 24-lake comparative dataset was assembled collaboratively across many contributing researchers rather than generated by the authors alone.

title: The Role of Climate Change in the Proliferation of Freshwater Harmful Algal Blooms in Inland Water Bodies of the United States
what: A peer-reviewed literature review (AMS article type "REVIEW") in Earth Interactions (Vol. 28, Issue 1) by D. Yvette Wiley (USGS South Central Climate Adaptation Science Center) and Renee A. McPherson (University of Oklahoma) that synthesizes prior published research on how climate-change stressors (warming, shifting pr

claims.c: Freshwater harmful algae and cyanobacteria blooms are increasing in frequency and intensity due to anthropogenic impacts, specifically nutrient loading in watersheds and engineered alterations of natural waterways.

Beyond nutrient loading, multiple physical factors shape freshwater conditions favorable to harmful algae/toxin-producing cyanobacteria, and a growing body of research indicates climate-change stressors are also shifting waterbody conditions to favor these taxa over other phytoplankton.

The review frames excess nutrients (from agricultural/urban runoff) as the primary driver of freshwater HABs, describing warmer air/water temperatures and more intense precipitation extremes as factors that influence bloom frequency and duration rather than as the root cause.
A 'bloom' (overgrowth of harmful algae/cyanobacteria) increases the opportunity for toxin exposure among humans, companion animals, livestock, and wildlife.
As waters warm and precipitation patterns change over time, human/animal exposure to these blooms is projected to increase.

State-level policies and approaches for monitoring and reporting harmful algae/cyanobacteria blooms currently vary widely across the U.S., and it is undetermined whether any tribes have specific policies on harmful algae blooms.

The paper's stated purpose is to synthesize research on algal blooms in U.S. inland freshwater systems, examine how climate change contributes to trends in bloom frequency or severity, and outline approaches states and tribes may use to monitor, report, and respond to these blooms.

This is a review/synthesis article (not primary field/lab research or a new statistical model) with a substantial bibliography, though the exact reference count is reported inconsistently across metadata aggregators (roughly 106 to 132 references).

title: The role of heterocytes in the physiology and ecology of bloom-forming harmful cyanobacteria
what: A laboratory ecophysiology study that experimentally compares two harmful, bloom-forming, heterocytous (nitrogen-fixing) cyanobacteria, Dolichospermum flos-aquae and Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii, which differ in where heterocytes form along the filament, growing replicated monocultures of each under a factorial nitro

claims.c: Dolichospermum flos-aquae and Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii are both harmful bloom-forming cyanobacteria capable of fixing atmospheric nitrogen in heterocytes, but they differ in heterocyte placement: Dolichospermum expresses heterocytes at fairly regular intervals along the filament, whereas Cylindrospermopsis expresses them only at the filament's end.

The study used replicated single-species (monoculture) cultures of each species grown under a factorial combination of nitrogen and phosphorus levels (each limiting or sufficient), i.e., simulated eutrophication scenarios.
Dolichospermum reached high biomass regardless of nitrogen or phosphorus supply, indicating it can bloom both under nitrogen limitation and under nitrogen sufficiency.

Cylindrospermopsis biomass was strongly dependent on external nitrogen supply: it reached high biomass only when nitrogen supply was high, and was 15-20 times lower in biomass when it had to rely on its own nitrogen fixation.
Despite having functional nitrogen-fixation capacity, Cylindrospermopsis blooms would be expected only under conditions of high total nitrogen availability in nature.

The two species differ in when they physically express heterocytes: Dolichospermum forms heterocytes only in treatments without supplied nitrogen, while Cylindrospermopsis forms heterocytes regardless of nitrogen availability.

Regardless of that difference in heterocyte expression, actual functional nitrogen fixation in both species occurred only when nitrogen was limiting, and fixation increased significantly when phosphorus concentration was higher.

Under no supplied nitrogen, filament length responded oppositely in the two species: Dolichospermum filaments were the longest, while Cylindrospermopsis filaments were the shortest, up to 13 times shorter than under nitrogen sufficiency.

The authors conclude that heterocyte expression is a good proxy for nitrogen fixation in Dolichospermum, and filament length is a good proxy for nitrogen fixation in Cylindrospermopsis, and that these eco-physiological responses help explain the two species' distribution along natural nutrient gradients.

title: Widespread global increase in intense lake phytoplankton blooms since the 1980s
what: A peer-reviewed Nature research article (Ho, Michalak & Pahlevan, 2019) that uses 30 years (1984–2013) of Landsat 5 satellite imagery to quantify long-term trends in the peak intensity of summertime, near-surface phytoplankton blooms across 71 large lakes in 33 countries on six continents, reporting a widespread global

claims.c: Using 30 years of Landsat 5 satellite imagery, peak summertime near-surface phytoplankton bloom intensity increased in a majority (68%) of the 71 large lakes studied worldwide.
A statistically significant decrease in bloom intensity was rare — occurring in only about 8% of the 71 lakes (reported elsewhere as six lakes) at the study's chosen significance threshold.

The study is a global analysis built entirely from 30 years (1984–2013) of 30 m-resolution Landsat 5 imagery for 71 large lakes across 33 countries and six continents, processed via a Google Earth Engine partnership handling over 72 billion image pixels.

The authors state they could not identify a consistent driver behind the widespread bloom-intensity increase: trends did not track consistently with temperature, precipitation, or fertilizer-use records, or other previously hypothesized causes.

Among lakes that showed improvement (a decrease in bloom intensity) at some point in the 30-year record, only those that warmed the least were able to sustain that improvement — an association suggesting lake warming may be offsetting nutrient-management gains, not proof that warming causes worse blooms generally.
Freshwater phytoplankton blooms are costly, causing more than US$4 billion per year in economic losses in the United States alone — cited as background/motivation, not a result this study generated.

A 2021 Nature 'Matters Arising' comment directly challenges this paper's core remote-sensing method, arguing a single near-infrared (NIR) reflectance band is not a reliable stand-alone bloom-strength proxy, that sparse Landsat 5 revisit frequency limits statistical power, that sediment plumes and submerged vegetation can mimic bloom NIR signals, and that the Fmask masking algorithm caused some severe scum-covered bloom pixels to be dropped as non-water.

Lakes with at least 14 years of data (n = 49 of the 71) were grouped into distinct multi-decadal 'historical pathway' bloom-trajectory categories (illustrated in Figure 2 with 5-year moving averages per lake and per-pathway averages).

title: A Review of Harmful Algal Blooms: Causes, Effects, Monitoring, and Prevention Methods
what: A 2025 open-access narrative literature review (Water journal, MDPI) by Brenckman et al. (NJIT) that synthesizes existing literature and case studies on the causes, ecological/health/economic effects, monitoring and detection technologies, and prevention/mitigation strategies for harmful algal blooms (HABs). It is a te

claims.c: HABs are framed as a growing environmental concern with adverse impacts spanning aquatic ecosystems, human health, and economic activity.
Bloom formation is attributed to a combination of nutrient enrichment, environmental factors, and hydrological conditions.
HABs produce toxins that threaten aquatic biodiversity, contaminate drinking water, and cause economic losses in fisheries and tourism.
Agricultural runoff, wastewater discharge, and industrial pollution are identified as the specific anthropogenic sources introducing excess nitrogen and phosphorus that fuel bloom formation.
Climate change is presented as an amplifying factor for HABs via altered precipitation patterns, rising water temperatures, and intensified coastal upwelling.
The review identifies satellite remote sensing, molecular biotechnology, and AI-driven predictive models as the effective monitoring/detection methods offering promise for early intervention.
Nutrient load reductions, bioremediation, and regulatory policy are named as sustainable management strategies that can help mitigate HAB effects.

Public awareness and community involvement (responsible agricultural practices, reduced waste discharge, conservation support) are described as playing a crucial role in preventing and managing HAB events.

The paper self-describes as a review of existing literature and case studies (not original data collection or modeling), concluding that comprehensive, interdisciplinary approaches are urgently needed to regulate HABs.
The publication's own keyword metadata confirms the review's scope spans HAB causes, impacts, monitoring/detection, mitigation, and public awareness/education.

title: Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii dominates under very low and high nitrogen-to-phosphorus ratios
what: A field mesocosm (2500-L limnocorral) manipulation experiment in a eutrophic lake that imposed three total-nitrogen:total-phosphorus (N:P) treatments — ambient (40:1), low (7:1), and high (122:1) — to test how nutrient stoichiometry shapes phytoplankton community composition and cyanotoxin (saxitoxin) production, cente

claims.c: The study manipulated nitrogen-to-phosphorus (N:P) ratios in field mesocosms to three treatment levels: ambient (40:1), low (7:1), and high (122:1), using 2500-L limnocorrals.
Initial nutrient concentrations in the mesocosms were approximately TN ~1360 µg/L and TP ~75 µg/L.

By the end of the experiment, the phytoplankton community had become dominated almost entirely by the nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii — the title finding is that this dominance occurred under BOTH the very low (7:1) and very high (122:1) N:P treatments, not only under classic N-limiting (low N:P) conditions.
Concentrations of the cyanobacterial neurotoxin saxitoxin were higher ("enhanced") under the two highest N:P treatments tested.

The initial phytoplankton community was diverse (reported as 18 genera, dominated by chlorophytes and cyanobacteria including Cylindrospermopsis) before converging to near-monodominance by C. raciborskii, and this convergence under both low and high N:P was described as contrary to predictions from ecological stoichiometry theory.

The authors' interpretation (per the search-tool summary) is that because C. raciborskii can dominate across such an extreme range of N:P ratios, short-term nutrient-ratio management (e.g., via fertilization to shift N:P) is unlikely to effectively control blooms of this invasive species.

tensions: This finding complicates a simple "manage N:P ratio to control cyanobacteria" narrative sometimes invoked in HAB literature. Classic ecological-stoichiometry theory predicts N-fixing cyanobacteria (like C. raciborskii) should be favored specifically under N-limited, LOW N:P conditions (where fixing atmospheric N2 confers an advantage), and disadvantaged at high N:P. This study instead found near-total dominance by C. raciborskii under the ambient (40:1), low (7:1), AND high (122:1) treatments alike — i.e., across the full extreme range tested —

title: Early Seasonal Increases and Persistence in Relative Abundance of Potentially Toxic Cyanobacteria: Concerning Impacts of Extended Ice-Free Periods in Northern Temperate Lakes
what: A bioRxiv preprint (posted 2022-12-21, version 1; no peer-reviewed journal version on record) by Cameron, Müller, Stone, Buttle, Leach, Webster and Emelko (University of Waterloo) reporting an 18-month (July 2018-January 2020), 10-event field survey of cyanobacterial community composition via 16S rRNA (V4 region) ampli

claims.c: The study observed proliferation of cyanobacteria across all seasons in three oligotrophic lakes, without the visible biomass accumulations conventionally associated with cyanobacterial blooms in eutrophic summer systems.

Warmer water temperature and high nutrient availability are described as the key characteristics generally associated with cyanobacteria occurrence, with blooms typically expected in summer in eutrophic systems.

Analysis of almost 40 years of lake ice-phenology records for TLW showed rising temperatures have led to significantly longer ice-free periods and aquatic growing seasons, with warming most pronounced in autumn via a significantly later onset of ice-on.

Cyanobacterial community composition in three interconnected TLW lakes was characterized using 16S rRNA (V4 region) amplicon sequencing across an 18-month field campaign (July 2018-January 2020) comprising 10 synoptic sampling events.

During winter under ice cover, photosynthetic cyanobacteria were low in abundance or occasionally absent, but a non-photosynthetic basal cyanobacterial lineage, Melainabacteria, was detected persisting through the ice-covered period.

Photosynthetic cyanobacterial populations reappeared in the water column immediately after ice-off, and abundance was especially elevated in lakes whose surficial geology and morphometry favor greater fine-sediment (and associated nutrient) supply.

The authors interpret their combined ice-phenology and community-sequencing results as showing that climate forcing of hydrological/biogeochemical processes, together with intrinsic landscape features, is associated with increases in the relative abundance of potentially toxic cyanobacteria over an increasingly longer part of the year in this temperate Canadian forest biome.

title: Extracellular polysaccharide synthesis in a bloom-forming strain of Microcystis aeruginosa: implications for colonization and buoyancy
what: A peer-reviewed primary-research paper (Scientific Reports, 2019) comparing a colony-forming, bloom-derived Microcystis aeruginosa strain (CHAOHU 1326, isolated from Chaohu Lake, China) against three unicellular M. aeruginosa reference strains, using morphology (light/SEM/TEM microscopy), EPS quantification and size-fr

claims.c: Under identical axenic laboratory culture conditions, bloom-derived strain Microcystis aeruginosa CHAOHU 1326 forms visible colonies (aggregating as thin layers on the medium surface), whereas three unicellular reference strains (FACHB-925, FACHB-940, FACHB-975) remain single-celled and well dispersed.

CHAOHU 1326 cells have a significantly larger mean diameter (4.14 ± 0.4 μm) than each of the three unicellular strains (1.83 ± 0.2, 2.36 ± 0.2, and 1.76 ± 0.2 μm for FACHB-925, -940, -975 respectively), p < 0.05.

Massive gas vesicles are regularly observed between the thylakoids of CHAOHU 1326 by electron microscopy; this ultrastructural feature was not detected in any of the three unicellular reference strains examined.
After 4 days of incubation, CHAOHU 1326 produces significantly more extracellular polysaccharide (EPS) than each of the three unicellular strains.
Within CHAOHU 1326's soluble EPS pool, low-molecular-weight carbohydrate (<1 kDa) dominates at 62.2% of total soluble EPS, while high-molecular-weight carbohydrate (>5 kDa) makes up less than 10%.

Comparative genomics shows the CHAOHU 1326 genome carries more genes encoding glycosyl transferases, isomerases, epimerases, and polysaccharide-biosynthesis proteins than three reference Microcystis genomes (NIES-843, PCC7806SL, TAIHU98), including six isomerase genes and five epimerase genes.

The CHAOHU 1326 genome contains a cluster of 12 genes predicted to encode gas-vesicle synthesis machinery, including three identical-sequence copies of gvpA (the major structural gas-vesicle protein), which the authors interpret as enabling rapid, high-volume synthesis of GvpA.

All 18 EPS-biosynthesis-related genes selected for testing, and all predicted gvp (gas-vesicle) genes, show detectable transcriptional activity in CHAOHU 1326 during late exponential growth phase, as determined by reverse-transcription PCR.

The authors interpret their combined morphological, biochemical, and genomic results as indicating that EPS synthesis and gas-vesicle-mediated buoyancy are held in a fine balance in CHAOHU 1326, proposing EPS synthesis itself (via density regulation) as an additional buoyancy-control strategy alongside gas vesicles.

The authors note an association between high EPS production and reduced growth rate in CHAOHU 1326, consistent with prior literature linking high EPS content/colony formation to lower specific growth rate.

The paper frames colony formation and floating colonies reaching the water surface as ecologically advantageous for bloom formation, associating larger colony size with faster migration/floating to the surface and greater resistance to heavy-metal stress, high-light inhibition, and zooplankton predation.

title: Extreme rainfall drives early onset cyanobacterial bloom
what: A field case study documenting how a single extreme-rainfall/flood event in a eutrophic Ontario reservoir (Conestogo Lake) triggered an atypically early, toxin-producing cyanobacterial bloom, tracked through paired in-situ water-chemistry, phytoplankton-community, and cyanotoxin sampling before and after the storm. It

claims.c: Following a 2017 extreme rainfall/flood event, Conestogo Lake (a eutrophic Ontario reservoir) developed an atypically early, toxin-producing cyanobacterial bloom rather than its usual late-summer bloom.

Bioavailable phosphorus in surface waters rose more than 27-fold after the flood, preceding a marked Aphanizomenon flos-aquae increase about 2 weeks post-event — roughly 5 weeks earlier than the reservoir's typical bloom onset.

The storm was hydrologically extreme: record daily rainfall (78 mm at Conestogo Dam; watershed-wide range 40-120 mm) on 23 June 2017 sharply raised inflow (from a baseline mean of 4.56 m3/s to 219 m3/s, peaking at 521 m3/s), added 3.5 Mm3 to reservoir volume, replaced ~80% of reservoir volume, and cut residence time to 6.04 days versus a normal range of 26-119 days (mean 43 days).

A. flos-aquae dominated cyanobacterial biomass (95-97% of the cyanobacterial fraction at all sites) and peaked at 10,900 mg/m3 (transitional zone, 19 July) and 4,710 mg/m3 (lacustrine zone, ~25 July), yet total cyanobacterial biomass overall (mean 1550 ± 2920 mg/m3) showed no significant relationship with sampling time or site.

Cyanotoxins (anabaenopeptin-A, microcystin-LR/-YR/-RR) were present through the 3-5 week bloom period, detectable in 87% of samples (n=13) and quantifiable in 73% (n=11); MC-LR was the dominant congener (highest zone mean 74.46 ± 156.5 ng/L, transitional zone), and total microcystin never exceeded the cited 20,000 ng/L recreational guideline.

The authors interpret the result as evidence that extreme rainfall can elongate the cyanobacterial bloom/toxicity season by shifting onset earlier, while stating explicitly that the magnitude of this effect is contingent on storm timing and reservoir physical structure.

The authors draw a forward-looking management implication: because extreme-rainfall events are projected to increase faster than total precipitation under climate change, managing blooms using historical/average conditions may become inadequate.

title: Harmful Algal Bloom–Associated Illnesses in Humans and Dogs Identified Through a Pilot Surveillance System — New York, 2015
what: A CDC Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report (MMWR) surveillance summary (Figgatt et al., MMWR 2017;66:1182–1184, DOI 10.15585/mmwr.mm6643a5) reporting the first season (June–September 2015) of a One Health pilot illness-surveillance system run by the New York State Department of Health in 16 counties to actively find,

claims.c: New York's 2015 pilot surveillance system logged far more HAB-associated illness reports in one 4-month season than the state's routine reporting had ever logged statewide in a full year, which the authors interpret as evidence of prior underreporting.
Of 51 HAB-associated illness reports collected by the pilot, 35 (68.8%) met CDC's case definitions: 32 human cases (1 confirmed, 20 probable, 11 suspected) and 3 canine cases (all probable).

Statewide across all of New York in 2015 (not limited to the pilot counties/season), 139 HABs were identified, 97 (70%) of them laboratory-confirmed, resulting in 77 separate beach closures at 37 beaches on 20 distinct water bodies.

Beaches with a visually identified HAB stayed closed to water contact until the bloom was invisible for at least one day and a commercial test strip showed cyanobacterial toxin concentration below a 10 microgram-per-liter guidance value.

Cases were classified using a three-tier CDC definition in which 'suspected' requires exposure plus symptoms plus a public-health plausibility judgment, 'probable' adds a medical diagnosis or observational/environmental lab data, and only 'confirmed' requires clinical HAB laboratory data.
Among the 32 human cases, skin problems (rash) were the most common symptom (22 patients, 69%), no human was hospitalized or died, and swimming was the dominant exposure route (28 patients, 88%).
All three canine cases had gastrointestinal symptoms; two of the three dogs were hospitalized and the third recovered without treatment, and none died.
The authors frame the higher case count in the pilot year as evidence of underreporting in prior (passive) surveillance rather than evidence that HAB illness incidence actually increased in 2015.

The authors state that HAB illness surveillance is inherently low-specificity because the associated signs and symptoms are nonspecific and there is often no data causally linking a given illness to HAB exposure.

The 16 pilot counties were not a representative sample of New York: all had already experienced at least one HAB in the prior season, so reported illness burden may reflect more HAB-aware communities rather than the state overall.

title: Increasingly severe cyanobacterial blooms and deep water hypoxia coincide with warming water temperatures in reservoirs
what: A peer-reviewed observational/statistical analysis of 32 years (1987–2018) of routine in-situ monitoring data from 20 temperate USACE reservoirs in the USA, testing whether cyanobacterial cell densities, water-column temperature, dissolved oxygen, and nutrients changed over time, and identifying which environmental var

claims.c: Cyanobacteria cell densities across 20 US reservoirs remained mostly low-to-moderate human-health risk until 2003–2005, then rose rapidly; since 2009, high-risk densities (>100,000 cells mL⁻¹) became frequent (75% of yearly observations), including 53% of observations from mostly-forested-watershed reservoirs, and by 2018 all 20 reservoirs had recorded blooms above that threshold.

Human-health risk was operationalized using WHO cell-density thresholds (low <20,000; moderate 20,000–100,000; high >100,000 cells mL⁻¹) rather than measured toxin concentrations, because cyanotoxins themselves were not monitored.

The rise in bloom severity coincided with progressively earlier and longer summer warming of surface waters, earlier onset of stratification, lengthening deep-water hypoxia, and warming of deep waters in non-stratifying reservoirs — a temporal coincidence rather than a manipulated causal test.

Deep-water dissolved oxygen declined across nearly all months and reservoir types between 1989 and 2018 (roughly 0.0 to about -2.1 mg/L depending on month/type per Table 1), and summer hypoxia now persists about two months longer in agricultural reservoirs than earlier in the record.

In stratifying reservoirs, three variables (summer precipitation z-scores, June surface water temperature, and surface-water total Kjeldahl nitrogen/TKN) jointly explained 56.3% of deviance in a GAM of cyanobacteria cell density (adjusted R2 = 0.534 per the associated figure caption) — an association among years, not proof of causation.

In weakly/non-stratifying reservoirs, the best available predictors (August deep-water temperature and June dissolved oxygen) explained far less variance — only 22% of GAM deviance — showing the statistical account of bloom drivers is markedly weaker for that reservoir subgroup.

Total nitrogen and phosphorus concentrations and watershed land cover were largely stable over the study period even as cyanobacteria densities rose sharply, suggesting the bloom increase in these reservoirs is not simply explained by increased bulk nutrient loading or land-use change.

Cyanobacteria are physiologically favored by warming because their growth rates peak at higher temperatures (25-35 degrees C) than other phytoplankton groups such as diatoms and cryptomonads, whose growth declines above roughly 15-25 degrees C — offered as a mechanistic rationale drawn from phytoplankton physiology, not a new experiment in this study.

Potentially toxin-producing taxa dominate these reservoirs' cyanobacterial communities: 9 of the 11 most abundant taxa recorded across all reservoirs and sampling events are possible toxin producers, and their densities increased since the early-to-mid 2000s.

Findings rest on a substantial long-term monitoring record: 20 USACE reservoirs, 1987-2018, with 775 sampling events at the deepest station and 23,043 cyanobacteria cell-density records spanning four depths (surface, 1.52, 3.05, 6.10 m).

The authors argue that reduced within-year sampling frequency after 2005 should, if anything, have made blooms and increasing trends harder to detect, implying the true increase is unlikely to be an artifact of more frequent recent sampling.

Data from targeted monitoring conducted since 2012 in response to publicly reported HAB events were deliberately excluded from the trend analysis to avoid bias from non-random, bloom-triggered sampling; only routine ambient monitoring data were analyzed.
Nutrient data were sparse, especially in 2000-2006, which limits confidence in the nutrient-trend conclusions relative to the temperature/dissolved-oxygen trend conclusions.

title: Nitrogen Limitation of Intense and Toxic Cyanobacteria Blooms in Lakes within Two of the Most Visited Parks in the USA: The Lake in Central Park and Prospect Park Lake
what: A five-year (2015–2019) nearshore water-quality/toxin monitoring record plus a detailed 2020 open-water sampling campaign and manipulative nutrient-addition/dilution bioassay experiments on two small, artificial, heavily-visited urban lakes in New York City parks (The Lake in Central Park [LCP] and Prospect Park Lake [

claims.c: Both lakes exhibited extremely elevated cyanobacterial biomass and microcystin over five years of nearshore monitoring, with cyanobacteria dominating phytoplankton biomass.
Cyanobacteria made up more than 99% of total phytoplankton biomass across both lakes from 2015 to 2019.
Nutrient-addition and dilution bioassay experiments showed that nitrogen, not phosphorus, consistently limited cyanobacterial growth, whereas green algae were rarely nutrient-limited.

DIN:DIP molar ratios in both lakes stayed well below the Redfield ratio of 5 all season and fell below 1 during the July–August bloom peak, indicating N was scarce relative to P exactly when blooms were most intense.

Absolute dissolved inorganic nitrogen concentrations, not just the N:P ratio, were low enough (below ~2 µM for most of the summer) to be growth-limiting on their own, since this is below the reported half-saturation constant for most bloom-forming cyanobacteria.

In manipulative bioassays, phosphorus addition alone never stimulated cyanobacterial or green-algal growth in LCP, while nitrogen addition (5 or 15 µM nitrate) significantly increased cyanobacterial growth in both lakes.

Public drinking water used to replenish these lakes during summer evaporation is comparatively rich in phosphorus and poor in nitrogen, and experimental addition of that drinking water to lake water significantly enhanced cyanobacterial growth rates.

Measured microcystin concentrations routinely and vastly exceeded recreational and health-based guideline levels (EPA 8 µg/L; WHO 20 µg/L 'moderate probability of adverse effects'; NY DEC >20 µg/L notification threshold), framed by the authors as a potential public-health concern given these parks' heavy visitation.
The microcystin-synthetase gene marker mcyE was significantly correlated with measured microcystin toxin concentrations in the 2020 dataset across both lakes.

The saxitoxin-synthesis gene sxtA was detected at high copy numbers (up to ~10^6 copies/mL), especially in PPL and in early summer, but actual saxitoxin toxin concentrations were not measured, leaving open whether saxitoxin itself reached concerning levels.

The very high nearshore microcystin/biomass values reported for 2015–2019 are not representative of open/offshore lake conditions: 2020 offshore sampling found levels generally orders of magnitude lower even though blooms still persisted all summer.
The specific sources supplying nitrogen to these urban lakes (sediment release, drinking-water supplementation, watershed/runoff loading) were not apportioned or quantified in this study.

title: pH-dependent gas vesicle formation in Microcystis
what: A laboratory molecular-physiology study showing that raising extracellular pH increases gas-vesicle abundance and cell buoyancy in cultured Microcystis, via reduced decay (increased stability) of gas-vesicle-protein (gvp) gene transcripts over a roughly 48-hour timescale. The primary URL (Wiley/FEBS Letters) is paywall

claims.c: Gas vesicle abundance and cell buoyancy in Microcystis both increase in response to elevation of extracellular pH.

Within 48 hours of a pH upshift, gas vesicle protein (gvp) genes are upregulated at both the mRNA and protein level, and the abstract attributes this specifically to reduced decay of gvp transcripts (i.e., a post-transcriptional/mRNA-stability mechanism) rather than describing it as increased transcription.
The pH effect on GvpC (gas vesicle protein C) level is essentially independent of inorganic carbon (Ci) availability, i.e., Ci does not appear to explain or confound the pH effect.

The authors frame this as the first report that long-term (sustained) pH range, specifically, controls gas vesicle formation in certain Microcystis species — an explicit novelty claim that is also self-bounded to 'certain' species, not Microcystis or cyanobacteria in general.
The environmental motivation for the study is that lakes with seasonal cyanobacterial blooms show pH fluctuating from slightly above 7 to around 10.

(Secondary, unverified) The pH-upshift experiment used a Microcystis culture referred to as 'Microcystis 930', grown in BG11 medium buffered with TES to a final concentration of 8 mM, comparing pH 7.0 versus pH 9.0; gvpA1 and gvpC transcripts were measured by qRT-PCR with gene-specific primers, GvpC protein by Western blot, and gas vesicles by electron microscopy.

(Secondary, unverified) Because full induction of GvpC took about 48 h, the authors reasoned that gas-vesicle abundance would not track short-term diurnal pH fluctuations in lakes, but instead responds to the lake's longer-term/seasonal pH regime — used to argue that long-term pH (not daily swings) is what is mechanistically linked to surface Microcystis bloom formation.

(Secondary, unverified, single-mention) Microcystis 930 reportedly carries three tandem gvpA gene copies that encode an identical protein but differ at the nucleotide (silent-substitution) level, which is given as the reason gene-specific primers were needed to isolate gvpA1 expression.

Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) concentration increased substantially along the inlet-to-outlet flow path; in July the outlet-site Chl-a was 8.1 times the inlet-site value, and cyanobacteria dominated phytoplankton cell density.

Dolichospermum spp. biovolume increased significantly under the warming treatment.

Pseudanabaena spp. presence and biovolume both increased significantly under nutrient enrichment.
Nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria genera dominated the overall cyanobacterial community by biovolume across the experiment.

The +4°C warming treatment used in the experiment sits within the upper range of IPCC-projected warming under the RCP8.5 scenario.
The primary cyanobacteria measurement tool (a submersible phycocyanin fluorometer) was cross-validated against, and correlated well with, independent microscope-based biovolume counts.

A fluorometer calibration/interface error meant that cyanobacteria chlorophyll-a data prior to May 2015 were compromised and were explicitly excluded from statistical modeling, even though they are shown and discussed descriptively.
Cyanobacterial cell densities reached under the study's warmed/nutrient-enriched conditions exceeded World Health Organization guideline thresholds for drinking and bathing water safety.

The authors explicitly bound the applicability of their findings to enriched, shallow lakes, stating that climate-change responses are expected to differ in deep lakes and that food-web factors not manipulated here (grazing, macrophytes) also shape real-world outcomes.

The authors caveat that their simulated flushing (extreme-rainfall) events were likely too infrequent and too short in duration to fully capture real extreme-rainfall impacts on lake phytoplankton, which may explain the limited long-term flushing effect observed.

Nutrient-enriched (hypertrophic) mesocosms achieved markedly higher total phosphorus and total nitrogen concentrations than ambient-nutrient mesocosms, which sat near the eutrophic-hypertrophic boundary.

title: Response of cyanobacterial blooms to climate warming: evidence from satellite observations and long-term trends in Lake Taihu in China
what: A peer-reviewed, open-access study (Scientific Reports, 2025) that fuses 20 years (2003–2022) of MODIS/Aqua and MODIS/Terra satellite imagery (NDVI-based cyanobacterial bloom detection, ~14,600 images) with in-situ hourly meteorological station data (5 ground stations run by the Jiangsu Climate Center) to quantify how

claims.c: Lake Taihu's annual average air temperature rose over 2003–2022 (mean 17.1°C), increasing by about 0.4°C per decade; linear trend Tyear = 0.04t + 16.86 (R = 0.49, p < 0.05).

The headline dose–response finding: for every 1°C rise in annual average temperature, annual cumulative satellite-detected bloom area is estimated to increase by about 5,377 km² (S = 5377.1T − 78931; R = 0.44, p < 0.05); cumulative bloom area itself trended upward by about 3,566 km²/decade on average.

The first observed bloom date (FOD) each year shifted earlier over the record — about 39 days/decade earlier on average (41 days earlier comparing the second half of the record to the first) — and is negatively correlated with winter–spring (Dec–May) mean temperature (FOD = −0.02·TWin-Spr + 12.55; R = −0.73), a temperature series that itself rose ~0.7°C/decade.

The last observed bloom date (LOD) each year shifted later — about 18 days/decade later on average (23 days later in the second half of the record vs. the first) — and is positively correlated with November–December mean temperature (LOD = 0.02·TNov-Dec + 2.34; R = 0.6); the Nov–Dec temperature trend itself was weak (TNov-Dec = 0.02t − 21.49, R = 0.11).
The annual cyanobacterial bloom season lengthened, from 238 days in the first decade of the record to 302 days in the second decade — a 27% increase in duration.

The relationship between temperature and bloom extent/frequency follows an empirical S-shaped curve: blooms are rarely observed below 5°C; area and frequency both exceed 10% of their annual totals at 15°C, rising above 20% at 20°C; both peak at 30°C (32% of area, 28% of frequency); and both decline sharply above 30°C.
The annual frequency (count) of satellite-detected bloom events shows a continuous increasing trend over 2003–2022 (Frequencyyear = 5.69t + 38.86).

2006–2007, 2017 and 2019–2020 were the most severe bloom periods in the 20-year record, with annual bloom areas roughly 30% above the long-term average; 2003, 2004, 2009 and 2014 had areas roughly 30% below average.

Bloom extent was mapped from 14,600 MODIS/Aqua and MODIS/Terra images (2003–2022, cloud-masked and 6S-atmospherically-corrected) using an NDVI-based threshold of −0.02, calibrated from 100 paired MODIS RGB/NDVI images (candidate thresholds ranged −0.0086 to −0.0325); 1,973 images retained a detectable bloom area ≥1 km².

The authors themselves caution that temperature alone does not fully explain interannual bloom variability — e.g., 2004 and 2016–2020 had similar average annual temperatures but markedly different cumulative bloom areas — attributing residual variation to other climatic factors, nutrient levels and human intervention, and stating no single climatic factor can comprehensively describe bloom dynamics.

title: Retention and Internal Loading of Phosphorus in Shallow, Eutrophic Lakes
what: A review/synthesis article — "a general overview of the nature and important mechanisms behind internal loading of phosphorus (P)... in shallow, eutrophic lakes upon a reduction of the external loading" (The Scientific World Journal, Vol. 1, pp. 427–442, published 23 Aug 2001; DOI 10.1100/tsw.2001.72). It explains why

claims.c: Internal P loading is a common phenomenon in shallow, eutrophic lakes following reductions in external P loading, such that lake water quality is not improved as expected.
After external load reduction, summer total-P concentrations can rise and whole-lake P retention can turn negative during most of the summer.
The internal P release originates from a phosphorus pool that accumulated in the sediment during the earlier period of high external loading.

In most lake sediments, P bound to redox-sensitive iron compounds and P fixed in labile organic forms are the major sediment-P fractions that are potentially mobile and can be released to the water column.
The recovery period after a reduction in external P loading depends on the lake's loading history and can last for decades where sediment P accumulation is high.
During the recovery phase, both the duration and the net P release rate from the sediment tend to decline progressively over time.
Internal P loading is strongly influenced by a lake's biological (food-web) structure, illustrated by lakes shifting from a turbid to a clearwater state, e.g. via biomanipulation.
In lakes that shift to a clearwater state, phosphorus concentrations may fall to about 50% of pre-biomanipulation levels, shortening the period of negative summer P retention.

Restoration methods such as sediment dredging (to remove accumulated P) or addition of iron or alum (to raise sediment P-sorption capacity) can significantly shorten the duration of internal P loading.

Long-term water-quality benefits from these restoration methods require a sufficient reduction in external P loading as a prerequisite; internal-loading treatments alone are implied to be insufficient without it.

title: Sediment Resuspension as a System-Wide Driver of Legacy and Bioavailable Phosphorus Release in Lake Erie
what: A 2026 peer-reviewed primary research article (Dhiman et al., Environmental Science & Technology) that combines in-situ sediment phosphorus fractionation, Beryllium-7 radiotracer analysis, and Sentinel-3 satellite-derived suspended-particulate-matter (SPM) retrievals to produce what the authors call "the first even

claims.c: During the observed resuspension event, sediments released bioavailable phosphorus (BioP) at 2.3–11 × 10⁻² g m⁻², which the authors calculate is 22–256× greater than previously reported aerobic diffusive P fluxes, establishing resuspension as a major episodic internal P source in western Lake Erie.

Scaling in-situ sediment P data to the whole basin via satellite-derived ΔSPM, the authors estimate the May 18–26, 2023 event mobilized 40,314 kg of total phosphorus (TP) basin-wide and released 12,665 kg of bioavailable phosphorus (BioP) as dissolved P to the water column, with resuspended sediment accounting for 89.2% of total resuspended material across the lake.

The single event's mobilized P equaled roughly 1.6% and 3.3% of the 2017–2021 mean Maumee River spring TP and SRP loads respectively, or roughly 4.7% (Results) / 4.8% (Abstract) and 7.0% of the current, reduced spring target loads for TP and SRP respectively.

Beryllium-7 (⁷Be) depth profiles show that a spring 2023 resuspension episode increased the sediment mixing rate (Db) roughly 5-fold at the near-river station WLE1 (14.5 to 70.51 cm² yr⁻¹) and roughly 12-fold at nearshore station WLE13 (5.43 to 69.6 cm² yr⁻¹), consistent with resuspension reworking multi-year sediment deposits and remobilizing legacy P.

The satellite SPM algorithm used to detect resuspension carries a validated uncertainty of NRMSE = 13.9%, and only pixel-level SPM changes exceeding this threshold were classified as resuspension events.

Satellite-derived erosion depth during the May 2023 event varied spatially: 1.39 cm at the shallow nearshore station WLE13 (the highest observed value), about 0.3 cm each at WLE14 and WLE16, with a basin-wide range of 0.2 to 0.8 cm across western Lake Erie.

Despite comparable satellite-estimated erosion depths at WLE14 and WLE16 (~0.3 cm each), only WLE14 showed a corresponding rise in dissolved SRP, showing that resuspension magnitude alone does not linearly predict local bioavailable-P response — the authors attribute WLE16's muted response to its peripheral position receiving water already depleted of BioP.

The authors state that most existing ecological and HAB-forecast models for systems like Lake Erie rely on external nutrient loading or constant diffusive-flux terms and do not capture this episodic, resuspension-driven internal P source, which they suggest is a gap relevant to explaining interannual bloom variability.

The paper operationally defines 'bioavailable phosphorus' (BioP) as the labile fraction of total sediment P obtained via sequential extraction: the loosely-bound, redox-sensitive, and Al/Fe-oxide-bound fractions (excluding Ca-bound and residual-organic fractions).

A third-party automated summary of this paper (not the paper's own wording) characterizes its overall conclusion as: light availability is an important driver of phytoplankton community structure, with a shift from communities dominated by P. agardhii in very turbid water toward more diverse communities -- potentially including the invasive C. raciborskii -- in clearer water.

title: The role of surface water waves on cyanobacterial blooms in lakes
