title: Toxic Effects Produced by Anatoxin-a under Laboratory Conditions: A Review
what: A 2022 peer-reviewed narrative literature review, published in the open-access journal Toxins (MDPI) and indexed on PubMed Central, that compiles laboratory in vitro and in vivo toxicological studies of the cyanobacterial neurotoxin anatoxin-a (ATX-a) in order to characterize its mechanisms of toxicity and dose–respons

claims.c: The review's explicit aim is to compile all in vitro and in vivo laboratory toxicological studies of anatoxin-a (ATX-a) performed to date and to identify data gaps, motivated by WHO's assessment that the existing database is inadequate for a formal guideline value.

WHO has issued only provisional (not formal) numeric reference values for ATX-a — 30 µg/L for acute/short-term drinking-water exposure and 60 µg/L for recreational water exposure — while explicitly stating the underlying toxicological database is inadequate to support a formal guideline.

ATX-a's mechanism of action is as a nicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist that causes membrane depolarization via the receptor channel, blocking neuromuscular transmission (with the introduction also describing acetylcholinesterase inhibition as part of the toxic mechanism).
ATX-a has been detected in a meaningful minority of algae-based dietary supplement samples, indicating a human dietary/supplement exposure route distinct from drinking water.
Among the water-body types surveyed in the literature the review draws on, reservoirs show the highest reported frequency of ATX-a occurrence, which the authors flag as a specific concern.

The authors conclude that ATX-a toxicological research remains far more limited than for other major cyanotoxins (e.g., microcystins, cylindrospermopsin), and specifically call for more standardized in vitro neuronal work.
The authors identify mutagenicity, genotoxicity, immunotoxicity, and hormonal-balance alteration as specific ATX-a toxicological endpoints that remain understudied and need deeper investigation.
Toxicokinetic data (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion) for ATX-a are described by the authors as scarce, limiting confident extrapolation from lab dosing to real-world exposure.
The authors state that very few of the existing in vivo studies reflect real-world/natural exposure conditions, and call specifically for longer-duration oral exposure studies.

Reported acute lethal-dose values for ATX-a vary by roughly an order of magnitude or more across species and administration routes (e.g., oral LD90 ~120 mg/kg in goldfish vs. ~1800 mg/kg in mice; intraperitoneal LD50 ~260–315 µg/kg in mice vs. ~60 µg/kg in rats), illustrating substantial interspecies and route-dependent variability in measured potency.

In vitro pharmacology data reviewed indicate ATX-a is consistently more potent than nicotine at nicotinic receptors across multiple neuronal/tissue preparations, with wide-ranging potency ratios reported between studies.

year: Page last reviewed Feb. 23, 2024; OHHABS system itself launched in 2016 (per page text)
what: OHHABS is a CDC-run, voluntary national public health surveillance system, launched in 2016, through which U.S. state and territorial health departments report harmful algal bloom (HAB) occurrences and the associated human and animal illnesses (including foodborne HAB-toxin exposures). CDC frames it explicitly as a 'On

claims.c: OHHABS is CDC's system for collecting data on harmful algal blooms and the human and animal illnesses they cause.
CDC launched OHHABS in 2016.
OHHABS is described by CDC as the only national public health system that collects information on both HAB occurrence and the human/animal illnesses HABs cause.
OHHABS operates under a 'One Health' framework, i.e., it is designed on the premise that human, animal, and environmental health are interconnected.
CDC states OHHABS data are meant to help define patterns of HAB occurrence, protect water and food supplies, and inform public communication to prevent future illness.

OHHABS collects reports in four data categories: harmful algal bloom occurrence/description (fresh, salt, or brackish water), food-source exposure (for HAB-toxin foodborne illness without an observed bloom, e.g. seafood harvest location), human illness cases, and animal illness cases (pets, livestock, wildlife).
A bloom can be logged in OHHABS on its own, without any associated illness report, if health departments have not identified any illnesses linked to it.
Reporting to OHHABS is done voluntarily by state and territorial public health departments, not directly by individual clinicians, veterinarians, or the public.
Health departments may designate environmental-health or animal-health partners to help with OHHABS reporting duties.
Healthcare providers and veterinarians are instructed to report HAB-associated illness cases to their local or state health department, not directly to CDC/OHHABS.

Public health, environmental-health, or animal-health professionals seeking to report to or access OHHABS should contact their state's OHHABS Reporting Site Administrator (RSA), reachable via a CDC email contact.

This page does not name any specific causative organisms (e.g., particular cyanobacteria genera or algal species) or specific toxins (e.g., microcystin, domoic acid); it refers only generically to 'harmful algal blooms' and 'HAB toxins.'
This page provides no quantitative statistics whatsoever — no bloom counts, no illness case counts, no number of participating states/territories, and no time-series or trend data.

title: Climate Change and Freshwater Harmful Algal Blooms
year: Not stated as an original publication year on the page. Page footer gives "Last updated on February 6, 2026" (confirmed verbatim across two independent fetches); no earlier publication/first-posted date is given in the fetched text.
what: An EPA HABs-portal web page that explains, in plain language for a general audience, the mechanisms by which climate change is expected to make freshwater (and adjoining estuarine/marine) cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (HABs) more frequent and severe when combined with nutrient pollution. It is a synthesis/explain

claims.c: Climate change effects on freshwaters, estuaries, and marine environments are already being documented by scientists and are predicted to worsen; combined with nutrient pollution, this may make algal blooms more severe and more frequent across more waterbodies.

HABs (blooms with potential to harm human health, pets, livestock, or aquatic ecosystems) can also have wide-ranging economic impacts; in freshwater, cyanobacteria are described as the most common HAB producers, and some cyanobacterial HABs produce toxins that cause illness in humans and other animals.

Cyanobacteria that form HABs thrive in warm, slow-moving water and typically bloom when water temperatures are warmer; climate-driven warming is expected to increase both the magnitude and the duration of cyanoHABs.

CyanoHABs gain a competitive advantage over other algae in warming water because they can grow faster, can migrate vertically to access nutrients from cool bottom layers while photosynthesizing in the warmer, light-rich upper layer (a trait other algae lack), and absorb sunlight/release heat in ways that further warm the surface water.

In some regions, climate change is predicted to reduce freshwater runoff via drought and increased evaporation, raising salinity in inland waters; this has allowed salt-tolerant "golden algae" HABs to recur regularly, expand into lagoons, and kill fish in freshwater lakes of the southwestern and south-central U.S. since 2000.

In other regions, more intense rainfall may increase freshwater flows to coastal areas; because freshwater is lighter and layers atop saltwater, this can prevent mixing between oxygen-rich surface water and oxygen-depleted deeper water (oxygen depleted by decomposition of dead HABs), producing hypoxic zones that harm or kill oxygen-dependent animals, including commercially important species.

Because algae require CO2 to survive, higher atmospheric and aquatic CO2 levels can drive rapid growth of algae, especially cyanoHABs that float to the surface; higher CO2 also raises water acidity, which affects competition among algal species and affects the organisms that graze on algae.

Climate change is increasing both rainfall intensity and drought duration; increased rainfall causes higher nutrient runoff into waterbodies, which fueled HABs observed in Lake Erie in 2011 and 2015, and subsequent drought lets waterbodies retain those nutrients longer, favoring species that compete well under high-nutrient conditions.

Increases in extreme rainfall and the resulting increases in freshwater flows may flush large loads of nutrients, freshwater HABs, and their associated toxins into estuaries and marine areas, raising the risk that those waters develop new HABs or see existing HABs worsen.
Scientists predict that sea level could rise one meter by the year 2100, which would increase the area of shallow, stable coastal waters that provide favorable conditions for HABs.

Climate change is expected to alter the timing and intensity of coastal upwelling; along the U.S. west coast, excess nutrients delivered by upwelling might lead to more algal blooms, including HABs, especially when combined with increased runoff of land-based nutrient pollution.
The page illustrates the topic with a real satellite image of a cyanobacterial HAB in the western basin of Lake Erie, captured October 8, 2022, credited to the European Space Agency.
The page cites no specific peer-reviewed study, government report, dataset, or statistical/mechanistic model as the evidentiary basis for any of its six climate-mechanism claims.

The advisory's toxicity basis is a single critical study — Heinze (1999), a 28-day rat drinking-water study — identifying a LOAEL of 50 µg/kg/day for microcystin-LR based on increased liver weight, liver lesions with hemorrhage, and elevated serum liver enzymes.
EPA applied a composite (total) uncertainty factor of 1000 to the LOAEL to derive the Reference Dose.
EPA uses microcystin-LR as a surrogate toxicant for total microcystins (roughly 100 known congeners) in setting this advisory, since MC-LR is the most potent and most-studied congener.
EPA states there is inadequate information to assess the carcinogenic potential of microcystins under its 2005 cancer-risk-assessment guidelines.

The main historical human health-effects evidence base for microcystins is acute recreational-exposure symptom reporting, plus a documented case series of intravenous exposure via microcystin-contaminated dialysate that caused acute liver failure and death in a large number of exposed patients; oral (ingestion) toxicity data in humans are limited and confounded.
This Health Advisory is explicitly non-regulatory: it is not a legally enforceable standard and confers no legal rights or obligations.

title: Impacts of Climate Change on the Occurrence of Harmful Algal Blooms
what: A May 2013 U.S. EPA Office of Water fact sheet (820-S-13-001) that synthesizes existing science on the mechanisms by which climate change — warming, salinity shifts, rising atmospheric CO2, altered rainfall patterns, sea level rise, and changing coastal upwelling — may promote the growth, dominance, and geographic spre

claims.c: EPA defines harmful algal blooms (HABs) as algal blooms that impair aquatic ecosystems or have the potential to affect human health, distinguishing them from ordinary (non-harmful) algal blooms.

The fact sheet states that scientists have observed an increase in the frequency, severity, and geographic distribution of HABs worldwide in recent decades, and frames climate change as a plausible contributor via six listed mechanisms (warmer water temperatures, changes in salinity, increased atmospheric CO2, changes in rainfall patterns, intensifying coastal upwelling, sea level rise).

Certain harmful cyanobacteria, exemplified by Microcystis, are described as growing faster than non-harmful algae at relatively high water temperatures, with some outcompeting above 77°F, which the source says increases the likelihood of HAB events.

Warming is said to increase the frequency, strength, and duration of thermal stratification, and stratified conditions favor cyanobacteria/dinoflagellates that can regulate buoyancy (e.g., via gas vesicles) to migrate and form dense surface blooms, gaining light/nutrient advantage over algae unable to reach the surface.
The fact sheet describes a positive feedback loop in which HABs absorb sunlight and warm surface water, which in turn further favors their own growth and competitive advantage.

Warmer water is said to have lower viscosity, which increases the speed at which small cyanobacteria can migrate to the surface (aiding bloom formation) while promoting sinking of larger, non-motile algae/organisms, increasing cyanobacteria's competitive advantage.

Climate-change-driven increases in drought intensity and duration are described as reducing lake/reservoir inflow and increasing evaporation, lowering water levels and raising salinity; this can both stress algal cells into leaking/releasing toxins and create conditions favorable to marine HAB species invading freshwater systems.

As a concrete example of marine-to-freshwater HAB invasion under elevated salinity, the source states that the marine golden alga Prymnesium parvum has been increasing in southwestern and south-central U.S. lakes since 2000, causing significant fish kills in inland waters.

Rising atmospheric CO2 is said to increase dissolved CO2 in aquatic systems, favoring algae that grow faster under elevated CO2, with surface-floating cyanobacteria singled out as having a distinct advantage because they can access atmospheric CO2 directly rather than relying only on dissolved CO2.

Decomposition of algal biomass (including bloom-derived organic matter) is described as consuming oxygen and adding CO2 in poorly circulated waters, contributing a local/ecological acidification effect on top of ocean acidification from atmospheric CO2; the source states these water-chemistry shifts can alter competitive relationships between HABs and other algae and can change zooplankton grazing control of HABs.

The source states that intensifying extreme rainfall (more concentrated bursts) followed by longer dry/drought periods, as projected under climate change, could increase nutrient runoff into water bodies and, if followed by drought, allow those nutrients to be retained longer, increasing the potential for HAB development.

The source cites projections that global sea level could rise 18 cm to one meter by 2100, which would expand continental shelf area and create shallow, stable coastal waters that may favor HAB growth or expand HAB habitat further inland.

The source notes that HAB response to coastal upwelling is variable (depending on atmospheric oscillations, water-column stratification, nutrient availability, and upwelling intensity/persistence), and that along ocean boundaries such as the U.S. west coast, climate-driven intensification of upwelling may deliver more nutrients to coastal waters, favoring growth of both harmful algal blooms and benign species, not HABs specifically.

The document itself states that although much of its presented evidence suggests HAB problems may worsen under future climate scenarios, further research is needed to better understand the climate-HAB association.

numbers: 77°F — water temperature above which certain harmful cyanobacteria (e.g., Microcystis) are said to grow faster than non-harmful algae (illustrative example; no baseline, error bar, or underlying-study citation given in t
18 cm to 1 meter — projected global sea level rise by year 2100, attributed only to unnamed 'scientific models' (range only; no point estimate, confidence interval, or emissions scenario specified)

since 2000 — period over which Prymnesium parvum (golden algae) is said to have been increasing in southwestern/south-central U.S. lakes (qualitative trend statement; no magnitude, rate, or count given)
EPA 820-S-13-001 (MC 4304T), May 2013 — document identifier and publication date, not a scientific measurement
tensions: Within the document itself, the coastal-upwelling mechanism is described as benefiting "both harmful algal blooms and benign species" — an explicitly non-selective effect that sits in tension with the fact sheet's broader narrative (temperature, viscosity, and CO2 mechanisms) that climate change gives HABs a specific competitive edge over non-harmful algae. More generally, because this is a 2013 qualitative mechanism/plausibility review that itself states the climate-HAB association "needs further research," it should be weighed in a literature

title: National Lakes Assessment 2022 Key Findings
what: The National Lakes Assessment (NLA) 2022 Key Findings page is EPA's summary of "The Fourth Collaborative Survey of Lakes in the United States" — a statistically-based (probability-sample) survey of lake condition across the conterminous U.S., run by EPA with states, tribes, and other partners as part of the National Aq

claims.c: Half of U.S. lakes are in poor condition for phosphorus and nearly half for nitrogen.
Nearly half of lakes have excessive (poor-rated) chlorophyll a, and 30% are hypereutrophic.
Lakes with elevated phosphorus show a statistical association with degraded benthic invertebrate communities, stronger in natural lakes than reservoirs.
Roughly a quarter to a third of U.S. lakes have degraded biological communities nationally.
Lakeshore disturbance and physical habitat degradation are widespread, and are linked to worse biological condition.
The cyanotoxin microcystin is detected in half of U.S. lakes, though only a small fraction exceed the recreational health criterion.
The cyanotoxin cylindrospermopsin is less prevalent than microcystin and did not exceed its health criterion anywhere sampled in 2022.
Microcystin detection increased sharply since the last survey cycle even though nutrient levels changed little.
The herbicide atrazine is widely detected at low levels, with benchmark exceedance rare but ticking up since 2017.
Mercury and PCB contamination in lake fish tissue is essentially universal nationally.
PFAS ('forever chemicals'), especially PFOS, are nearly ubiquitous in lake fish tissue nationally.
The 2022 cycle is the fourth in EPA's repeating national lake probability-survey series, and this page's trend analysis only goes back to 2017.

title: Recommendations for Cyanobacteria and Cyanotoxin Monitoring in Recreational Waters, Revision 1 (EPA 823-R-19-001)
what: A September 2019 EPA Office of Water guidance document (EPA 823-R-19-001, Revision 1) that lays out a voluntary, non-binding four-step framework — assess vulnerability/prioritize, observe for blooms, monitor for cyanotoxins, follow up — to help recreational-water managers and public health officials decide whether a bl

claims.c: EPA frames the document around a voluntary four-step conceptual monitoring program: (1) assess vulnerability and prioritize waters, (2) observe for blooms throughout the season, (3) monitor for cyanotoxins, (4) follow up.
EPA explicitly states the document's recommendations are not legally binding, do not create legal rights, and do not substitute for Clean Water Act provisions or EPA regulations.
EPA (citing Loftin et al., 2008) characterizes 'extremely high' cyanobacterial bloom cell densities as being above roughly 20,000 to 100,000 cells/mL.

The source associates exposure to elevated cyanobacterial cell densities with dermal effects (skin rash), ear/eye infections, and gastrointestinal distress, stated as an association rather than a described causal mechanism.

Table 1 sets EPA's 2019 recommended recreational-water cyanotoxin criteria at two magnitude thresholds (8 µg/L and 15 µg/L, one for microcystins and one for total cylindrospermopsin), assessed over 10-day periods, with a frequency trigger of more than 3 'excursions' per recreational season recurring across more than one year indicating the water is not supporting recreational use.

WHO (2003) guidance (Table 2), used by several U.S. states for swimming advisories, defines four relative-probability tiers from 'Low' (<20,000 cyanobacteria cells/mL, <10 µg/L chlorophyll a, <10 µg/L microcystin) to 'Very High' (>10,000,000 cells/mL, >5,000 µg/L chlorophyll a, >2,000 µg/L microcystin); the microcystin values in this table were derived from the cell-density values rather than measured independently.
Visual/field observation cannot confirm whether cyanotoxins are present: unsafe toxin levels can occur with no visible bloom, so only laboratory testing of the water confirms cyanotoxin presence.
Cyanotoxin production is not a fixed or predictable property of a bloom — it is highly variable and depends on environmental conditions and which cyanobacterial species is present.

NOAA satellite imagery is already used operationally to help predict blooms in western Lake Erie and the Gulf of Mexico, and the NOAA/EPA/USGS/NASA CyAN partnership aims to build a nationwide early-warning cyanobacteria indicator from satellite data — but the source states this approach is unsuitable for all waterbodies because typical satellite resolution and revisit frequency are limited.

Across the reviewed cyanotoxin detection methods (ELISA, HPLC with MS/UV-PDA, PPIA, LC/MS, qPCR/microarrays, microscopy), the source states no single method is best: chromatographic methods are accurate but congener-limited (may underestimate total risk), PCR-based methods are rapid but may overestimate risk if toxin genes are present without toxin release, and ELISA kits (the most commonly used field method) lack congener specificity and can cross-react.

As an example of risk-based monitoring prioritization, Utah's environmental agency identified 16 'sensitive' water bodies for close swim-season monitoring, selected using designated use, proximity to population, number of recreational users, and history of blooms.

numbers: "Extremely high" cyanobacterial bloom density (Step 1, citing Loftin et al., 2008): >20,000 to 100,000 cells/mL — no error bar given, stated as a typical definitional threshold rather than a measured statistic.

title: Surveillance for Harmful Algal Bloom Events and Associated Human and Animal Illnesses — One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System, United States, 2016–2018
what: A CDC MMWR Surveillance Summary reporting the first three years (2016–2018) of data from the One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System (OHHABS), the first U.S. national public health surveillance system that links reports of harmful algal bloom (HAB) events with associated human and animal illness cases, voluntarily submit

claims.c: OHHABS is a CDC-launched (2016) national, One Health, multisectoral public health surveillance system designed to link HAB event reports with human and animal illness data to inform prevention efforts.
Over the 2016–2018 period, 18 states reported a combined total of 421 HAB events, 389 human illness cases, and 413 animal illness cases to OHHABS.

Most reported HAB events were laboratory/field-confirmed, but the large majority of human illness cases were only "probable," and the large majority of animal illness cases were only "suspected" — fewer than 5% of illness cases (human or animal) were laboratory-confirmed.
HAB events and associated illnesses were heavily concentrated in freshwater bodies and in the warm-weather months (May–October), peaking in July.

Among events where a toxin was identified, microcystins were by far the most frequently detected toxin; other cyanotoxins (anatoxin-a, saxitoxin, cylindrospermopsin, nodularin) and the marine toxin ciguatoxin were detected much less often.

A single large freshwater HAB event — a July bloom at one large lake that spread to connected waterways over more than three months — accounted for over half of all human illness cases reported system-wide during the three-year period.

No human deaths were reported among the 389 human illness cases, whereas 89% of the 413 reported animal cases died, with wildlife (chiefly birds) driving most of that animal mortality, including one large single-event bird die-off.
Very few ill persons or animals received confirmatory clinical/veterinary testing, and time from a single-point exposure to illness onset was generally short (median hours, not days).

The authors state that reported HAB event/illness counts substantially underrepresent the true national burden because participation and surveillance capacity vary across the reporting jurisdictions, and this was still the initial data-collection period.

166 event reports (39%) noted visible scum
372 events (88%) had lab testing performed
tensions: This is passive, voluntary surveillance from only 18 of 50 U.S. states, explicitly stated by the authors to undercount true incidence — so its absolute counts (421 events; 389 human and 413 animal cases) should not be read as a national HAB-illness rate or compared directly against remote-sensing-based bloom-extent products (e.g., EPA CyAN, which detects far more cyanobacterial blooms across many more water bodies/states than are captured here), since the gap reflects reporting/ascertainment limits, not necessarily true geographic/temporal diff

title: Field and Laboratory Guide to Freshwater Cyanobacteria Harmful Algal Blooms for Native American and Alaska Native Communities (USGS Open-File Report 2015-1164)
what: A 2015 USGS Open-File Report (2015-1164, DOI 10.3133/ofr20151164) by Rosen and St. Amand that serves as a field-and-laboratory identification guide to freshwater cyanobacteria harmful algal blooms, developed specifically to help Native American and Alaska Native subsistence-fishing communities recognize and distinguish

claims.c: The guide's stated rationale is that Native American and Alaska Native communities relying on subsistence fishing face an increased risk of cyanotoxin exposure, motivating a visual field/lab identification guide to distinguish toxic cyanobacterial blooms from non-toxic blooms.

Cyanobacteria are described as producing six major recognized groups of toxins — microcystins, saxitoxins, anatoxins, cylindrospermopsins, nodularins, and dermatoxins — each typically representing a family of structurally related derivative compounds.

Because a field sample is usually a mixture of organisms, attributing a detected toxin to one specific cyanobacteria species is inherently uncertain; the only reliable attribution method (isolating and culturing a single organism) is itself described as difficult and logistically burdensome.
Cyanobacteria are reported (via cited literature) to generally proliferate at water temperatures above roughly 25°C and to be more tolerant of these warm conditions than competing green algae.

Warmer surface waters are described as promoting vertical stratification, which is said to favor buoyancy-regulating cyanobacteria over other (eukaryotic) algae; the report frames global warming as a possible contributor to increased cyanobacterial bloom growth.

An algal bloom is described as forming under a specific combination of environmental conditions — nutrient abundance, water-column stability, ample light, and optimal temperature — with cyanobacteria identified as the algae type posing the greatest HAB problem.

Most bloom-forming cyanobacteria can actively regulate their buoyancy to position themselves in the water column and float to the surface, and wind can further concentrate phytoplankton accumulations along downwind shorelines.

Visual bloom coloration alone is explicitly stated to not indicate which cyanobacteria genus is present; the guide recommends repeated sampling plus microscopic confirmation to improve field identification confidence.
The presence or absence of taste-and-odor compounds (also produced by many cyanobacteria) is explicitly stated to not be predictive of whether algal toxins are present.

Human cyanotoxin exposure through consumption of fish and shellfish is described as poorly documented, resting on only a handful of studies measuring toxin concentrations in specific food organisms (e.g., microcystins in shrimp/catfish/carp, nodularins in mullet, saxitoxins in freshwater mussel).

Bloom coloration observed in the field is tied to specific pigments: phycocyanin (blue) can turn water/shorelines turquoise or chartreuse when released from ruptured cells, while phycoerythrin (red) is associated with brown, red, or pink bloom coloration.

The guide's Table 2 compiles literature-cited toxin associations for 26 illustrated cyanobacteria taxa, noting some species produce multiple toxin types and that exhaustively cataloguing all toxin-producing genera/species in the wider literature is explicitly out of scope.

numbers: ~25 degrees Celsius: temperature threshold above which cyanobacteria are reported (citing Robarts and Zohary, 1987) to proliferate and gain competitive advantage over green algae; no confidence interval or original sampl
26 cyanobacteria taxa: number of genera/species illustrated in the guide's Microscope Images section and covered in the Table 2 toxin-association compilation
44 p.: total length of the report per its own suggested-citation block
2015: publication year; Open-File Report 2015-1164; DOI 10.3133/ofr20151164 (bibliographic identifiers, not empirical data)
tensions: This source is a taxonomic/field-identification guide, not a remote-sensing or statistical modeling study, so it does not itself discuss or test satellite-based detection frameworks (e.g., EPA CyAN). It does, however, state two caveats that bear on any HAB-risk workflow relying on color or pigment proxies: 'Coloration is not an indicator of the genus of the organism(s)' and 'the presence or absence of taste and odor compounds is not predictive of the presence of algal toxins.' title: Learn about Harmful Algae, Cyanobacteria and Cyanotoxins
what: An official U.S. EPA educational/reference webpage (part of EPA's HABs program) that introduces and defines harmful algal blooms, cyanobacteria, and the major cyanotoxin classes (microcystins, cylindrospermopsins, anatoxins/guanitoxins, saxitoxins, nodularins), their general health/ecological effects, and the federal r

claims.c: EPA defines Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) as algal growths that harm human health and the environment, arising from excessive biomass, toxin production, or environmental degradation such as hypoxia/anoxia or acidification.
Cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) are microscopic, photosynthetic organisms common to freshwater, estuarine, and marine waters.

Cyanotoxins are produced under specific environmental conditions (light intensity, nutrients, temperature, salinity) and are released mainly when cyanobacterial cells die and rupture (lysis), not necessarily continuously during growth.

Microcystins are described as the most common cyanobacterial toxins, with over 200 known congeners (microcystin-LR most studied); they are primarily hepatotoxic, with some evidence linking microcystin-LR to liver and colorectal cancers, though EPA states current evidence is inadequate to assess carcinogenic potential.

Cylindrospermopsins have three known environmental congeners and act primarily on the liver and kidney, with potential to increase cellular stress, affect development, and cause DNA damage; EPA again states insufficient evidence to assess carcinogenicity.

Anatoxins/guanitoxins are neurotoxins that bind neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, with effects ranging from loss of coordination to death by respiratory failure; EPA states there is insufficient information on their carcinogenicity.

Saxitoxins are potent neurotoxins representative of the Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP) toxin family, with health effects ranging from minor tingling/numbness to death from respiratory failure; no carcinogenicity information is available for this class per EPA.
Nodularins are produced by cyanobacteria of the genus Nodularia, which primarily thrive in salty or brackish coastal or inland waters (rather than typical freshwater lakes).

Beyond direct toxicity, HABs cause ecological and economic harm through hypoxia/anoxia or acidification, unsightly water conditions, taste-and-odor problems affecting recreation/tourism/drinking water, and (for marine taxa such as K. brevis) large die-offs of fish, birds, and marine mammals including dolphins and manatees.

Regulatory responsibility for HABs in the U.S. is split by statute: the Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Act (HABHRCA) assigns NOAA responsibility for marine and Great Lakes HABs, while EPA holds responsibility for other freshwaters and separately has regulatory authority over marine waters affected by HABs under the Clean Water Act.

The page itself is an introductory/educational reference, not a technical or primary-data source: it names toxin classes and general contributing conditions but supplies no quantitative exposure thresholds, advisory concentrations, temperature/nutrient ranges, or monitoring data, instead pointing to separate EPA Health Effects Support Documents, Drinking Water Health Advisory documents, and state/tribal program links for that information.

3 congeners of cylindrospermopsin found in the environment: cylindrospermopsin, 7-epicylindrospermopsin, and 7-deoxycylindrospermopsin (count, not a concentration)

No concentration-based thresholds, health-advisory levels (e.g., µg/L or ppb), temperature ranges, or nutrient (N/P) concentration limits are stated anywhere on this page — the page explicitly defers to separate EPA Heal

It does, however, complicate any simple "toxin present = health risk" framing used elsewhere in a HAB literature base: EPA explicitly says evidence for microcystin-LR carcinogenicity is 'inadequate' and for the other three toxin classes ranges from 'insufficient' to 'no information,'

title: Toxins and Harmful Algal Blooms Science Team | U.S. Geological Survey
year: Undated program page (content cites findings spanning 2013–2019+)
what: A USGS Environmental Health Program science-team page describing the Toxins and Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) Science Team, whose mission is to "develop advanced methods to study factors driving algal toxin production, how and where wildlife or humans are exposed to toxins, and ecotoxicology" so as to build decision tool

claims.c: The team's mission is to develop advanced methods to study factors driving algal toxin production, exposure pathways for wildlife and humans, and ecotoxicology, in order to build decision tools assessing whether exposure causes adverse health effects.

The actual health threats posed to the public, pets, livestock, and wildlife by algal toxins in waters used for recreation and drinking water remain poorly understood, despite blooms occurring frequently in U.S. water resources.

A 2016 USGS assessment found microcystins (a cyanotoxin) in 39 percent of small streams assessed across the southeastern United States; the page headlines this same finding as toxins detected in roughly 'one-third' of streams assessed.

In a 2013-15 Lake Erie study, cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins were not associated with mussel mortality at the concentrations observed, but mussel growth was lower at sites with higher microcystin concentrations.

At Kabetogama Lake, toxin-producing cyanobacteria (or genes indicating toxin-synthesis capability) and cyanotoxins were detected both before and after the formation of a visible algal bloom — toxin presence was not confined to the period when a bloom was visually apparent.
An objective scientific review concluded that the hypothesis linking BMAA (a cyanobacterial toxin) to neurodegenerative diseases such as ALS and Parkinsonism is not supported by existing data.
The team's affiliated Algal and Environmental Toxins Research Laboratory (Organic Geochemistry Research Laboratory, Lawrence, Kansas) operates from a 2,500-square-foot facility built in 2019.
A phytoplankton data release documented on the page covers sampling at eleven large river sites across the United States, conducted June through September 2017.
The team states that a single bloom-detection metric (e.g., a satellite/remote-sensing bloom-extent measure) cannot by itself adequately represent how severe a bloom is in terms of toxicity.
Data releases associated with the team's work explicitly state that values are reported raw and not rounded to standard USGS significant-figure conventions.
Decisions to restrict public access to water bodies due to algal blooms are often driven largely by perceived risk rather than measured/actual risk.

numbers: 39 percent of small streams assessed in the southeastern U.S. tested positive for microcystins (2016 USGS finding; the page's own headline separately rounds this to 'one-third')

title: Cyanobacterial toxins: microcystins — Background document for development of WHO Guidelines for drinking-water quality and Guidelines for safe recreational water environments (WHO/HEP/ECH/WSH/2020.6)
what: A 2020 WHO background document that reviews toxicokinetic, human, and animal toxicological evidence on microcystins (a family of cyanobacterial cyclic-peptide hepatotoxins) to derive provisional health-based guideline values for microcystins in drinking-water and recreational water, replacing WHO's 2003 microcystin-LR-

claims.c: WHO's provisional guideline values (GVs) for microcystins are 1 µg/L (lifetime drinking-water), 12 µg/L (short-term drinking-water) and 24 µg/L (recreational water), derived from a mouse study of MC-LR corroborated by a similar rat study, with the liver identified as the most sensitive organ.

The short-term drinking-water GV is meant only to bound temporary exceedances (up to 2 weeks) while treatment is upgraded, not repeated seasonal exceedance of the lifetime GV, and is derived for adults; bottle-fed infants and small children are recommended an alternative water source if concentrations exceed 3 µg/L, even briefly.

Microcystins are described as the most commonly reported cyanobacterial toxins worldwide, produced mainly by freshwater cyanobacteria with wide geographic/ecological distribution; drinking-water is identified as the most likely human exposure route, with recreational contact during blooms as another potentially high-concentration pathway, and limited data suggesting some food accumulation.

The document attributes high cyanobacterial biomass to nutrient loading from anthropogenic sources (agricultural runoff, wastewater) and, on that basis, recommends nutrient-source control as the primary long-term bloom-management lever.

Conventional drinking-water treatment (coagulation, flocculation, filtration, chlorination) is generally sufficient to bring microcystins to acceptable levels; where it is not, ozonation and activated carbon (filtration or powdered addition) can be effective supplementary steps.

Mechanistically, microcystins are actively imported into cells via organic anion transport proteins (OATPs); because the liver highly expresses OATPs, microcystins act primarily as hepatotoxins, though other OATP-expressing tissues can be affected. Intracellularly they bind protein phosphatases with high affinity, disrupting cytoskeletal, cell-replication, stress-response and DNA-repair pathways, producing acute intrahepatic haemorrhage and, chronically, impaired tumour-proliferation control.

Because the GVs are derived from the single congener MC-LR while environmental exposure is generally to mixtures of MC variants, the document states that comparing total MC concentration (gravimetric or molar) to the GV is 'likely' to be protective in most circumstances — a hedged, not absolute, extrapolation.

The document notes an open scientific disagreement: some recent studies suggest microcystin effects on reproductive organs at lower concentrations than those underlying the liver-based GVs, but these findings are contradicted by earlier studies and are described as requiring corroboration.

Microcystins are cyclic heptapeptides first characterized in the early 1980s (named for Microcystis aeruginosa); more than 250 distinct variants have been identified, with molecular weights of 882–1101 Da; most are hydrophilic and highly water-soluble, with saturation concentrations several orders of magnitude above the WHO guideline values; microcystins and the related nodularins are also described as extremely heat-stable.

numbers: Lifetime drinking-water guideline value (provisional): 1 µg/L microcystin
Short-term drinking-water guideline value (provisional): 12 µg/L
Recreational water guideline value (provisional): 24 µg/L
Precautionary threshold for bottle-fed infants/small children: 3 µg/L (alternative water source recommended above this even for short periods)
Short-term GV validity window: up to 2 weeks (temporary, until treatment augmented; not for repeated seasonal exceedance)
>250 distinct microcystin variants identified to date
Microcystin molecular weight range: 882–1101 Da

No explicit numeric uncertainty factors, confidence intervals, or point-of-departure/TDI values are given in the extracted excerpt itself (that quantitative derivation is in Section 8.1, outside the provided window); GVs

what: Chapter 4 ("Understanding the occurrence of cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins") of the WHO reference book "Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water," 2nd edition — a review chapter synthesizing the physiological traits (nutrient storage, nitrogen fixation, buoyancy regulation, light harvesting, carbon concentration, grazing and viral

claims.c: The chapter explicitly cautions that presence of cyanobacteria does not automatically mean a health risk, and that assuming a tight, general link between eutrophication and cyanobacterial/toxin risk is too simplistic: some taxa (e.g., Planktothrix rubescens) tend to decrease under eutrophic conditions, while others (e.g., Microcystis) can reach hazardous levels at much reduced nutrient concentrations if a relatively low biomass concentrates into shoreline scums.

Cyanobacteria store surplus phosphorus intracellularly as polyphosphate in amounts exceeding immediate demand, letting a cell undergo multiple divisions after external P supply is exhausted below detectable levels.

Only a subset of cyanobacterial genera (e.g., Aphanizomenon, Dolichospermum) can fix atmospheric N2 ("diazotrophy") via specialized oxygen-free cells (heterocytes), giving a competitive edge over eukaryotic microalgae when other nitrogen sources are scarce; non-fixing genera such as Microcystis and Planktothrix can still benefit indirectly from nitrogen leaking from co-occurring N-fixing taxa.
Nitrogen fixation is energetically costly (heterocytes must maintain anoxic internal conditions while surrounded by oxygenated water), and the chapter states this process is limited in turbid water.
Thermal stratification, a key physical precondition for buoyant cyanobacteria to concentrate near the surface, is given a specific quantitative definition by the chapter.

Buoyancy in gas-vesiculate cyanobacteria is described as regulated most importantly by light (more gas vesicles produced under low irradiance; vesicles diluted by growth or collapsed by high turgor pressure under high irradiance), producing diel vertical migration with populations most buoyant at night and losing buoyancy during the day.

Different bloom-forming taxa show characteristically different vertical distribution patterns in stratified waterbodies: surface-bloom/scum formers (Microcystis, Dolichospermum, Aphanizomenon), near-homogeneous water-column distributions with only exceptional scum formation (Planktothrix agardhii), and deep metalimnetic/deep-chlorophyll-maximum layer formers (red-pigmented Planktothrix rubescens in deep stratified lakes; green-pigmented Aphanizomenon flosaquae and Dolichospermum reported to form metalimnetic layers lasting months; Raphidiopsis/Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii in deep, low-light layers of stratified subtropical reservoirs).

The chapter's own schematic (Figure 4.2) illustrates that, for a hypothetical buoyant Microcystis-type population, wind-driven physical concentration alone (independent of any biomass growth) could in principle span roughly five orders of magnitude in cell density and toxin concentration between a fully-mixed "low risk" state and a wind-concentrated shoreline scum "very high risk" state — but the chapter explicitly flags these numbers as illustrative only, not measured or reference values.

Resistance of cyanobacteria to zooplankton grazing is inconsistent across studies and remains scientifically debated: some grazers (e.g., Daphnia carinata) have been observed feeding on toxic Microcystis, while other experiments found daphnids preferentially avoided toxic Microcystis in favor of green algae/diatoms; outcomes appear to depend on the grazer population's prior exposure/co-evolutionary history with cyanobacteria.

Cyanophage (virus) infection is documented as one possible mechanism of Microcystis bloom termination and toxin release, but the same host genus showed the opposite outcome (lysogeny, prolonging bloom persistence) in a different lake in the same bloom year, so the chapter does not present viral lysis as a universal or reliably predictable driver.

numbers: One cyanobacterial cell can complete 8–16 cell divisions using stored intracellular polyphosphate without any further external phosphorus uptake (dimensionless count; Reynolds, 2006, as cited).
Thermal stratification (limnological definition used by the chapter): water-temperature difference of more than 1 °C per 1 m of depth.

Figure 4.2 illustrative wind-concentration schematic, explicitly labeled 'crude estimates...must not be taken as reference values': (a) fully-mixed/'low' risk ≈ 10^7 cells/L, chlorophyll-a 2 µg/L, microcystin (MC) 1 µg/L
Assumed average toxin cell quota underlying the Figure 4.2 estimates: 100 fg microcystin (MC) per cell (chapter's Box 4.8).
Raw-water microcystin concentration spike at Toledo, Ohio's drinking-water intake, associated with a 2014 Lake Erie Microcystis cyanophage lytic event: >5 µg/L (Steffen et al., 2017, as cited).

tensions: The chapter's central framing caveat — that a tight link between eutrophication/nutrient loading and cyanobacterial or toxin risk is "too restrictive" because taxa and physical concentration mechanisms (e.g., wind-driven scum formation) can decouple toxin risk from nutrient status — is in tension with simpler HAB screening approaches that use nutrient concentration or trophic state alone as a proxy for bloom/toxin risk; this source suggests such proxies could under- or over-state risk depending on taxa and physical concentration dynamics. title: Toxic Cyanobacteria in Water: A Guide to Their Public Health Consequences, Monitoring and Management
what: A WHO-commissioned edited reference guide (1st edition, 1999; eds. Ingrid Chorus and Jamie Bartram) that synthesizes expert consensus on cyanobacterial ecology, cyanotoxins, and human/animal health effects into operational guidance for protecting drinking-water and recreational water bodies. Per its own Table of Conten

claims.c: The only known human fatalities associated with cyanobacteria and their toxins occurred via renal dialysis exposure in Caruaru, Brazil, killing over 50 patients.
Non-fatal human poisoning outbreaks attributed to toxic cyanobacteria have been reported in Australia (contaminated drinking water) and the UK (army recruits exposed while swimming and canoeing).

Severe acute human-health effects from cyanotoxins appear rare, but the scale and nature of long-term effects (e.g., tumour promotion, liver damage) and milder short-term effects (e.g., contact irritation) remain poorly understood.
The guide's content was produced via a defined sequence of WHO-convened international expert meetings and reviews rather than a single original study.
Total phosphorus concentrations below 0.1 mg/L are described as sufficient to induce a cyanobacterial bloom.
A regional lake survey found that 54% of Asia-Pacific lakes, 53% of European lakes, 28% of African lakes, 48% of North American lakes, and 41% of South American lakes were classified as eutrophic.

Decline in cyanobacterial blooms is described as lagging behind reductions in phosphorus inputs, because phytoplankton growth only becomes nutrient-controlled once concentrations fall below a threshold.
Bloom formation is framed as jointly dependent on temperature, light, and nutrient status together, not on nutrient level alone.

Temperate waters typically show a seasonal phytoplankton succession (diatoms/small flagellates in winter-spring, then green algae, then grazing-resistant taxa such as dinoflagellates, desmids and large yellow-green algae in late summer-autumn, with cyanobacteria often dominating summer phytoplankton in eutrophic/hypertrophic waters), whereas in the tropics seasonal environmental differences are often insufficient to displace cyanobacteria the way they are displaced in temperate systems.

The guide's full scope (per its own Table of Contents) spans cyanobacterial ecology and toxin classification, human/animal poisoning and toxicology, safe exposure levels, a risk-management framework including an "Alert Levels Framework," policy/legislation implementation, preventative measures (including phosphorus target values), remedial measures (including algicides and drinking-water treatment efficiency for cyanotoxin removal), and monitoring-programme design plus laboratory analytical methods for cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins.

numbers: Total phosphorus < 0.1 mg/L (mg P L⁻¹): stated as sufficient to induce a cyanobacterial bloom (Ch. 1.1); no confidence interval, sample size, or supporting citation given in the excerpt for this specific figure.

title: Welcome to the IOC Harmful Algal Bloom Website (GlobalHAB Programme)
year: n.d. (footer copyright © 2021; page content continuously updated, includes events/courses dated through 2027)
what: The official website of the IOC-UNESCO Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) Programme - an institutional portal (not a research paper) that describes the Programme's mission, its governing body IPHAB (IOC-FAO Intergovernmental Panel on Harmful Algal Blooms), the IOC-SCOR GlobalHAB research initiative, the HAIS/HAEDAT information

claims.c: The Programme's own framing statement asserts HABs as a worldwide coastal problem: every coastal country is potentially affected.
HABs are described as capable of killing marine life and causing human death.
The Programme's stated mandate is to help IOC member states build capacity to understand HAB causes, predict occurrence, and mitigate effects.
Concretely, this capacity-building takes the form of training in species identification, toxicity testing, and monitoring/management strategies.

The Harmful Algal Information System (HAIS) is the Programme's central data system: taxonomic-name data, HAB event/biogeographic information, an expert directory, and (added in 2021) an algal-toxin database.
The Global HAB Status Report (launched June 8, 2021) is described as the first-ever compiled overview of HAB events and their societal impacts worldwide.
Harmful Algae News (HAN) has been published as an IOC newsletter since 1992, covering toxic/harmful algal blooms, socio-economic impacts, research, and management activities.

IPHAB is characterized as "the only global intergovernmental effort" to understand, manage and mitigate HAB effects, and became jointly cosponsored with FAO (as IOC-FAO IPHAB) effective November 1, 2021.
IPHAB's 17th session (IPHAB-XVII) was held March 18-20, 2025 at UNESCO Headquarters in Paris; its 18th session (IPHAB-XVIII) is scheduled for March 16-18, 2027 at FAO Headquarters in Rome.

GlobalHAB (IOC-SCOR) is presented as the Programme's international collaborative-research arm, intended to advance international collaboration, improve predictive capability, and inform science and policy, organized around three focus areas (training; the international research programme; manuals & guides) with a Scientific Steering Committee and sub-committees.

As an applied, country-level example, UNESCO/IOC and Morocco's National Institute of Fisheries Research (INRH) began a joint project (workshop December 5-8, 2022) to develop a national HAB early-warning system.

The Programme's regional structure spans multiple ocean basins through named regional HAB networks: HANA (North Africa), IOC/WESTPAC-HAB, IOCAFRICA/HAB, ANCA (under IOCARIBE, Caribbean), and FANSA (South America).

The Publications page lists at least 31 distinct HAB-related technical publications spanning 1997-2026, including numbered institutional series such as IOC Manuals and Guides No. 88 and No. 59, IOC Technical Series No. 59 and No. 41, IOCCG Report Series No. 20, PICES Scientific Report No. 59, and FAO Fisheries and Aquaculture Technical Paper No. 551.

The Programme is hosted by the UNESCO/IOC Project Office for IODE in Oostende, Belgium (contact: hab.ioc@unesco.org), with a footer copyright dated 2021 even though the live site displays events and courses scheduled through 2027.

title: Cyanobacteria & Algal Blooms (Topic Page)
year: Not applicable — dynamic/continuously updated hub page; linked items span 2014-08-17 to 2025-10-06; accessed 2026-07-01
what: A topic/tag hub (content-aggregation) page on the Cary Institute of Ecosystem Studies website that lists and links to the institute's own news features, press releases, blog posts, a podcast, and media coverage related to cyanobacteria and harmful algal blooms (HABs), with items dated from 2014 to 2025. It functions as

claims.c: The page itself is a curated navigational hub of Cary Institute content about cyanobacteria/HABs, not an original research article — it presents no independent findings, data, or methodology.
The page links to a 2017 press release indicating Cary Institute researchers used a NASA grant to develop technology for studying toxic cyanobacteria.
The page links to a 2018 feature associating Landsat 8 satellite imagery with algal bloom monitoring.
The page links to two 2025 items (a feature and a press release) describing an extensive, recent harmful algal bloom in the Hudson River estuary and framing recurrence of such blooms as expected.
The page links to 2021 media coverage reporting that an algal bloom caused a reservoir near Beacon, NY to be taken offline.
The page links to two Translational Ecology blog posts framing basic ecological mechanisms relevant to HABs: legacy phosphorus and hypoxia (dead zones).
The page links to a 2017 media item reporting that scientists developed a warning system intended to reduce harm from toxic algal blooms.

numbers: $1.47M (USD) — NASA grant amount, appearing only in the headline of a 2017-10-17 press-release link title on this hub page; no methodological detail, baseline, or uncertainty is given for this figure on the page itself
tensions: No direct tensions are identifiable because the hub page contains no analytical or quantitative claims to compare against other HAB literature.
